questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Balance of Payments (BoP)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Balance of Payments (BoP),
which of the following are considered receipts in the Current Account?
I. - Purchase of patent
II. 	 Inward remittances
III. 	 Unilateral transfers from abroad
IV. 	 External Commercial Borrowings (ECB)
V. 	 Interest payments received by Indian residents on loans made to non-residents
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Balance of Payments (BoP)** is a systematic record of all **economic transactions** between residents of a country and the **rest of the world** during a specific period. The **Current account** records transactions relating to goods, services, income, and current transfers. And inflows of foreign exchange into the country are recorded as receipts.
● The **purchase of a patent** from abroad involves **payment to a non-resident** for an **intangible** **asset.** This results in an **outflow of foreign exchange** and is therefore recorded as a **debit entry** under the Capital account. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **Remittances** sent by Indians **working abroad** to residents in India **bring foreign currency** into the country. These are **unilateral transfers** without any quid pro quo and are recorded as **receipts** in the Current account under current transfers. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Unilateral transfers from abroad** are one-way payments like **gifts, grants, and aid received** from foreign entities without any reciprocal service or good. They are standard components of the current account receipts. **So, point III is correct.**
● **External Commercial Borrowings (ECB)** represent **borrowing** from non-residents and lead to an increase in **external liabilities.** Such transactions are **recorded in the Capital account,** not the Current account, even though they bring foreign exchange into the country. **So, point IV is not** **correct.**
● **Interest income** earned by Indian residents on **loans extended to foreign entities** is treated as **primary income** from abroad. This inflow is recorded as a **receipt** in the Current account. **So, point** **V is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Economic Survey 2025–26 highlights that Gross Fixed Capital Formation has maintained a share of about 30% of GDP in FY26, after declining to around 29.6% in FY25.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: GDP, Inflation, Unemployment
questions.questionText: If a country’s Gross Fixed Capital Formation (GFCF) rises but its change in inventories falls by the same amount, Gross Capital Formation will:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Capital Formation** refers to the net addition to an **economy’s stock of physical assets** during a given period and is a key component of **Gross Domestic Product (GDP)** under the expenditure approach. **Gross Capital Formation (GCF)** comprises **three elements**: **Gross Fixed Capital** **Formation (GFCF)**, changes in **inventories (stocks),** and **acquisition** of valuables.
● **GFCF** represents **investment in fixed assets** such as buildings, machinery, equipment, infrastructure, **intellectual property** products, and cultivated biological resources that are used repeatedly in **production over several years.** It is termed “gross” because it does not deduct depreciation or consumption of fixed capital. An increase in GFCF indicates **higher investment** activity and **potential** **future productive capacity.**
● **Changes in inventories,** on the other hand, capture the **variation in the value of unsold goods** held by producers, wholesalers, or retailers over a period. An increase in inventories **adds to capital** **formation,** while a decline implies that previously produced goods are being sold, **reducing capital** **formation** for that period.
● **Gross Capital Formation** is calculated as the sum of GFCF, changes in inventories, and acquisition of valuables. Therefore, if GFCF **increases by a certain amount** but the change in inventories **decreases** by exactly the **same amount**, the two effects offset each other. Since the **net addition** **remains unchanged,** Gross Capital Formation remains the same. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Gross Fixed Capital Formation as a percentage of GDP is at 33.7% in 2024-25.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Financial Markets
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** India’s corporate bond market remains underdeveloped and relatively illiquid compared to developed economies.
**Statement II:** Around 98% of corporate bond issuances in India take place through private placements. **Statement III:** The corporate bond market is overwhelmingly dominated by AAA-rated issuances.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **a**
**Explanation:**
● India’s corporate bond market remains **underdeveloped/shallow** (in terms of retail participation) **and** **illiquid** when compared to developed economies. This reflects the broader structure of India’s financial system, which is **bank-dominated rather than market-based**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● According to the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI), **around 98% of corporate bond** **issuances in India are conducted via private placements**, which are primarily accessed by institutional investors and are not available to the general public. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ **Private placements are largely buy-and-hold instruments**, which **do not actively trade** **in the secondary market**, directly reducing liquidity.
● The **low share of corporate bonds in total corporate financing** limits market depth, issuer diversity, and investor participation.
● According to **the Economic Survey, 2025-26,** India’s corporate bond market is largely **dominated** **by AAA-rated issuances**, accounting for nearly **80 percent,** which account for a substantial majority of total bond issuances. This concentration indicates that only highly rated corporates are able to access the bond market easily, while lower-rated firms face constraints. Such skewed distribution limits credit diversification, risk pricing, and active secondary trading, thereby contributing to the narrow investor base and structural underdevelopment and illiquidity of the corporate bond market. **So, statement III** **is correct.**
● As a result, despite growth in outstanding bonds, **secondary market turnover remains far below** **that of the G-Sec market**, reinforcing illiquidity. **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About the Corporate Bonds:**
● **Corporate bonds** are debt instruments through which companies raise loan capital from investors, acknowledging an obligation to repay the principal along with interest over a specified period.
● Unlike equity, bondholders are lenders rather than owners; they do not enjoy voting rights or profit participation but have a **higher claim on assets** than shareholders in the case of insolvency.
● Credit rating is mandatory for listed corporate bonds under SEBI regulations and acts as a key signal of default risk, strongly influencing investor demand and pricing.
● The inverse relationship between bond prices and yields governs trading behaviour in the secondary market.
● They may be fixed-rate, floating-rate, zero-coupon, perpetual, or market-linked debentures.
● They can be issued either through **public issues or private placements**.
**Relevance:** According to the Economic Survey, 2025-26, over 98 % of corporate bond issuances in India are done through private placements rather than public issues, showing that most companies prefer the faster, less cumbersome private route for raising debt.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Balance of Payments (BoP)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Over the past five years, direct taxes have contributed a higher share than indirect taxes to the Centre’s gross tax revenue.
II. 	 Dividends and profits transferred by public sector enterprises and the Reserve Bank of India have constituted the largest component of non-tax revenue in the past five-year years.
III. 	 In the post-pandemic years, external borrowings have been the principal source of financing the fiscal deficit.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Over the **post-pandemic period (FY22–FY26)**, the share of **direct taxes** (corporate tax + noncorporate income tax) has **consistently exceeded indirect taxes**. Direct taxes rose from **about**
**51.9%** pre-pandemic to nearly **58.8% in FY25**, driven by better **compliance**, **formalisation**, **higher** **personal incomes**, and improved corporate profitability. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Among **non-tax revenue** components, **dividends and profits**, especially surplus transfers from the **Reserve Bank of India and public sector enterprises**, have become the dominant source in recent years. For instance, dividends and profits **jumped sharply in FY24–FY26**, far exceeding interest receipts and service fees.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **largest source of fiscal deficit financing** in the past five years has been **internal market** **borrowings via dated government securities**. Gross market borrowings rose sharply post-COVID, reaching about **₹****14.8 lakh crore in FY26** and projected **₹****17.2 lakh crore in FY27**, while external debt remained negligible (around 1% or less). **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Economic Survey 2025-26 was recently released by the Ministry of Finance, projecting a robust GDP growth of 6.8%–7.2% for FY27.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Monetary Policy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the revised Consumer Price Index (CPI) in India, consider the following statements:
I. - The year 2024 has been adopted as the new base year.
II. 	 The weight of food and beverages has been reduced, while that of housing has been increased.
Which of the statements given above is/are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Consumer Price Index** (CPI) measures changes over time in the retail prices of a weighted basket of goods and services consumed by households, reflecting their relative importance in consumption expenditure. It serves as the principal indicator for inflation targeting under India’s monetary policy framework.
● The Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI) has **revised the CPI base year** **from 2011–12 to 2024**, using data from the **2023–24 Household Consumption Expenditure** **Survey (HCES).** This update makes the index more representative of **current consumption** **patterns** and income levels. **So, statement I is correct.**
● In the **new CPI basket,** the weight of **food and beverages** has **fallen** from about **45.86% to 36.75%,** reducing volatility caused by food price shocks. At the same time, **housing weight has increased** sharply (around **10% to nearly 17.7%**), reflecting higher rent and utility expenditure. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
○ Major categories in the CPI basket typically include food and beverages, housing, clothing, transportation, medical care, education, and fuel/light. ○ **New Additions to the Basket:** Rural housing; online media service providers/streaming services; value-added dairy products; barley and its products; pen drives and external hard disks; attendant services; babysitter services; and exercise equipment. **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**
**Relevance:** According to documents released by the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI), India’s revised Consumer Price Index, with 2024 as the base year, will significantly lower the weight of food and beverages from 45.86% to 36.75%.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Balance of Payments (BoP)
questions.questionText:
With regard to the Digital Rupee (e₹), consider the following statements:
I. 	 It is a decentralised virtual currency similar to cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin.
II. 	 It is a legal tender issued by the Reserve Bank of India, with a value at par with physical currency.
III. 	The balance held in an e₹ wallet earns interest or profit like a savings bank account.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Digital Rupee (e****₹****)** is **India’s Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC),** issued by the Reserve Bank of India. It represents a **digital form** of **sovereign currency**, combining the features of physical cash with the convenience of **digital payments.**
● The Digital Rupee is **not decentralised.** Unlike private cryptocurrencies that rely on **distributed** **consensus** mechanisms, e₹ is **centrally issued**, **regulated**, and controlled by the RBI, making it a sovereign currency **backed by the State** and governed within the formal monetary system. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● The Digital Rupee is a **legal tender** under **Section 26 of the RBI act, 1934**, guaranteed by the Government of India. It is issued **at par with physical rupee notes and coins** and can be used for payments, transfers, and as a store of value. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Balances held in an e₹ wallet **do not earn interest or profit.** The Digital Rupee is designed to have **cash-like features,** similar to physical currency notes, which also do not yield interest. This distinction prevents **disintermediation of bank deposits** and protects financial stability. **So, statement III is** **not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** As of early 2026, the retail pilot of the Digital Rupee (e₹) has expanded to over 82 lakh (8.2 million) users and 11 lakh merchants.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Agriculture (Schemes, APMC, MSP, Exports, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The recent increase in the Female Labour Force Participation Rate in India in 2025–26 indicates a structural shift towards high-productivity and formal employment.
**Statement II:** The rise in female participation is largely concentrated in rural areas, particularly within self-employment and agricultural activities.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation for Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is **not** the correct explanation for Statement I.
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS)** conducted by the **National Statistical Office** **(NSO),** **Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI)**, is the primary source of data on activity participation and employment-unemployment conditions of the population. It provides **monthly and quarterly** estimates of labour force indicators for both **rural and urban India**.
● As per the recent  **Female Labour Force Participation Rate (FLFPR)** trends in India **(2025-26)**, it was observed that the **rise is concentrated in rural agriculture and self-employment**, while urban regular salaried employment grew only "modestly," confirming that the trend is **not driven by** **high-productivity employment**. **So, statement I is not correct**.
● The rise in **rural FLFPR** is largely due to **increased participation of women as** **unpaid family** **workers and seasonal agricultural labour** as “seasonal agricultural operations” shows a significant spike in “self-employed” status (which includes unpaid family labor). **So, statement II is Correct**.
● The reliance on seasonal agriculture and self-employment (often unpaid) is evidence that participation is a result of **rural economic dynamics/distress rather than a structural shift to better jobs**. **So, Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** As per the statistics ministry’s latest data, India’s Labour Force Participation Rate ended 2025 at a new high of 56.1%.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Exchange Rates (NEER, REER, Currency Mechanisms)
questions.questionText:
With reference to rules governing the External Commercial Borrowings (ECB) in India, consider the following statements:
I. - Borrowers cannot simultaneously raise both foreign currency-denominated ECB and Indian rupee-denominated ECB.
II. 	 Indian residents can subscribe to bonds issued in overseas centres by eligible ECB borrowers.
III. 	 Indian banks cannot subscribe to rupeedenominated bonds issued overseas in the primary market.
Which of the statements given above is/are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **External Commercial Borrowings (ECB)** refer to the borrowing of funds **by** **Indian companies** **from foreign sources** in the form of **loans, bonds, or other financial instruments**. ECB can be in the form of **rupee-denominated loans or foreign currency-denominated loans**, which are repaid in a **foreign currency**. The **ECB** falls under the umbrella of **RBI regulations** and the **Foreign** **Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999**.
● **Entities** raising **Rupee-denominated ECB** are strictly **prohibited from converting** those liabilities into **foreign currency** or using derivatives to shift currency risk to foreign exchange.
● **Eligible borrowers may simultaneously raise** **both Foreign currency and Indian rupee-** **denominated ECB**, provided they follow RBI guidelines. The total individual borrowing limit encompasses the combined value of all such loans. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Indian residents are generally prohibited from subscribing to ECB bonds abroad, whether** **in foreign currency or Indian rupee**. Authorised Dealer (AD) banks must prevent any direct or indirect exposure for residents, excluding permitted exceptions like overseas branches of Indian banks. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Indian banks cannot subscribe to Rupee Denominated Bonds (RDB) issued overseas in the** **primary market,** but can be arrangers/ underwriters/ market makers/ traders, subject to compliance with prudential norms. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Indian companies have slowed down their fundraising through external commercial borrowings (ECBs) amid higher hedging costs.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
With reference to the advent of Europeans in
India, consider the following ports:
I. - Surat
II. 	 Masulipatnam
III. 	 Pondicherry
IV. 	 Goa
V. 	 Chandernagore
Which of the above ports were established as
factories/settlements by the English East India
Company?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **English East India Company**, established in **1600**, set up trading factories in India with royal and Mughal permissions, **focusing on maritime trade** in textiles and spices before evolving into a territorial power.
● **Surat** became the **first English factory on the Western Coast** of India in **1613** under Mughal approval. It was a major port for textile exports and served as the Company’s initial headquarters in western India. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Masulipatnam** was the **first English factory on the Eastern Coast**, established in **1611**. It was an important centre for **exporting cotton textiles** and played a key role in the Company’s trade with Southeast Asia. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Pondicherry** was founded by the **French East India Company in 1674** and became the **headquarters of French possessions** in India. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Goa** was captured by the **Portuguese from the Sultan of Bijapur, Ismail Adil Shah,** in **1510** and functioned as the capital of Portuguese India. It was a major **naval and administrative centre**, unrelated to the English East India Company. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Chandernagore** was established by the **French East India Company** in **1690** on the **Hooghly** **River.** It emerged as a key centre of French trade in Bengal, especially in textiles, and was not an English port. **So, point V is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Global Groupings and Agreements (FTA, Paris Agreement, NPT, QUAD, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to Muhammad Ali Jinnah, consider
the following statements:
I. - He criticised the separate electorate
introduced by the Morley-Minto Reforms
(1909).
II. 	 He earned the title “Ambassador of Hindu-
Muslim Unity” for his role in the Lucknow
Pact (1916).
III. 	 He resigned from the Indian National
Congress owing to disagreements with
Gandhi’s approach to mass movements.
IV. 	 Under his presidency, the Muslim League
passed the Lahore Resolution demanding a
separate nation for Muslims.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Muhammad Ali Jinnah** was a prominent nationalist leader who later became the chief spokesman of the **Muslim League** and the driving force behind the **demand for Pakistan,** shaping India’s partition politics.
● During the early phase of his political career as a moderate nationalist, **Jinnah opposed the system** **of separate electorates** introduced under the Morley–Minto Reforms of 1909. He regarded the Reforms as a “divide and rule” strategy that could deepen divisions between Hindus and Muslims. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Jinnah played a **major role** in the **Lucknow Pact (1916)**, which was a historic agreement between the **Indian National Congress** and the **Muslim League.** It accepted separate electorates and marked a phase of **Hindu–Muslim unity,** earning Jinnah the title **“Ambassador of Hindu-Muslim Unity.”** **So, statement II is correct.**
● Jinnah **resigned** from the **Indian National Congress in 1920**, mainly due to disagreement with **Gandhi’s Non-Cooperation Movement**, which he considered unconstitutional and based on mass agitation rather than legal and **parliamentary methods.** After this, he focused more on Muslim League politics. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The **Lahore Resolution (1940)** was passed by the **All-India Muslim League** under Jinnah’s leadership. It **demanded “independent states”** for Muslims in the north-western and eastern zones of India, which later evolved into **the demand for Pakistan. So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
The Butler Committee on princely states was
appointed by the British Government.
**Statement II:**
Political mobilisation in the princely states was on
the rise demanding responsible government.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is the correct explanation
for Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II is **not** the correct explanation
for Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Butler Committee (1927)** was appointed to examine and clarify the nature of the **relationship** between the **British Government** and the **princely states**. It emphasised that **British paramountcy** must remain **supreme** and that **princely states** could not be **transferred** to a responsible Indian government without their **consent**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● At the same time, **political awakening** and **mobilisation** in princely states was **increasing**, and organisations like the **All India States’ Peoples’ Conference** later demanded **democratic reforms** and responsible government. **So, Statement II is correct.**
● However, the Butler Committee was mainly concerned with **defining the constitutional position** of princely states and safeguarding **British paramountcy**. Its appointment was **not a response to the** **growing political mobilisation** or demands for responsible government within the states. **So,** **Statement II does not correctly explain Statement I.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Mahatma Gandhi described Section 124A of
the Indian Penal Code as “the prince among the
political sections of the IPC designed to suppress
the liberty of the citizen” in which of the following
contexts?

options.a:
While addressing the Ahmedabad Mill
Workers’ strike in 1918
options.c: While launching the Quit India Movement in
options.d:
Sedition trial following the Non-Cooperation
Movement in 1922

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mahatma Gandhi** made this remark during his **1922 sedition trial** at Ahmedabad, following the **Non-** **Cooperation Movement.** He had been charged under Section 124A of the Indian Penal Code for articles published in **Young India.**
● In his written statement before the court, Gandhi described Section **124A as “the prince among the** **political sections”** designed to suppress civil liberty. He pleaded guilty but used the occasion to articulate his opposition to colonial rule. He was subsequently sentenced to six years’ imprisonment. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Communal Award (1932), consider the following statements:
I. - It provided separate electorates for marathas, sikhs and marginalised women.
II. 	 It made provisions for the representation of labourers and industrialists.
III. 	 It introduced a system of double voting for the depressed classes, one in separate electorates and another in general electorates.
IV. 	 It reserved 10% seats for women in all provincial assemblies.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Communal Award (1932),** announced by **Ramsay MacDonald**, expanded **separate** **electorates** and reserved seats for minorities, including Depressed Classes. It aimed at **communal** **representation** but was criticised as a divide-and-rule policy and later led to the **Poona Pact.**
● In the Communal Award, **Muslims, Europeans, Sikhs, Indian Christians, Anglo-Indians**, **depressed classes, women**, and even the Marathas were to get **separate electorates.** Such an arrangement for the depressed classes was to be made for a **period of 20 years. So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The Award introduced **special representation** for economic and occupational groups. Seats were allocated for **labourers**, **landlords**, **traders**, and **industrialists**, reflecting the British approach of **dividing political representation** not only by community but also by class interests. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● Under the award, the **Depressed Classes had a ‘double vote,’** voting in their separate electorate for reserved seats as well as in the general electorate. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The Communal Award reserved **3% seats for women** in **all provinces except** the **North-West** **Frontier Province (NWFP). So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText: The title Siladitya is associated with which one of the following Indian kings?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The title **Siladitya** is associated with **Harshavardhana**, the **7th-century ruler of North India**, as recorded by the **Chinese Buddhist monk Xuanzang (Hsuan Tsang)** in his work **Great Tang** **Records on the Western Regions.**
● Xuanzang describes Siladitya as a **powerful yet benevolent king** who **unified large parts of** **northern India**.
● Harshavardhana was known for his **administrative diligence,** patronage of **Buddhism** alongside **Shaivism** and **Vaishnavism**, construction of stupas and monasteries, and generous **support to** **monks and Brahmins.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the architecture of India:
I. - Brihadeeswara Temple was constructed majorly of sandstone.
II. 	 Qutub Minar is built entirely of red sandstone.
III. 	 Khajuraho Temples are predominantly built of granite.
IV. 	 Hoysaleshvara Temple was built using soapstone.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Brihadeeswara Temple** represents the **zenith of Dravidian temple architecture under the** **Chola dynasty**, and is entirely constructed of **granite (not sandstone)**. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
○ The structure was built **without the use of mortar**, relying instead on precisely cut granite blocks fitted together through an interlocking system. ○ The temple complex is enclosed by **two concentric prakarams**, entered through **gopurams**.
● The construction of the Qutub Minar began under **Qutbuddin Aibak**, the founder of the Delhi Sultanate, who completed only the **first storey**. The remaining **three storeys** were added by his successor **Iltutmish** around **1232 CE**, completing the original structure. During the reign of **Firoz** **Shah Tughlaq**, the minar was **damaged by lightning**, leading to the destruction of the upper storey. Firoz Shah replaced it with **two smaller storeys**, giving the monument its present **five-storeyed form**.
○ The **first three are made of red sandstone,** the **fourth and fifth use marble.  So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● The **Khajuraho Group of Temples** is located in the **Chhatarpur district of Madhya Pradesh**. The Temples are believed to have been built of **light-coloured sandstone** imported from the quarries of Panna, from the east bank of the Kane River. Iron clamps are also freely employed in the construction of the temples. A few other smaller temples are built partly of **sandstone and partly of granite. So,** **statement III is not correct.**
○ Built by the **Chandela Dynasty**, the complex includes both **Hindu and Jain temples**, reflecting the pluralistic spirit of the period. Khajuraho is recognised as a **UNESCO World** **Heritage Site**. ○ Following the **decline of the Chandela dynasty** in the 13th century, after invasions led by **Qutb-ud-din Aibak**, many temples were destroyed. They were **rediscovered in the 1830s** **by British officer T.S. Burt**, leading to their restoration and renewed recognition.
● The **Hoysaleshvara Temple** is located at **Halebid in Karnataka**. The temple is dedicated to **Hoysaleshvara (Lord of the Hoysalas)** and is constructed using **dark schist (soapstone)**, which enabled exceptionally fine and intricate carvings. **So, statement IV is correct.**
○ It is often described as **Vesara or hybrid style**, as it does not conform strictly to either the **Dravida** or **Nagara** architectural traditions, but combines elements of both. ○ A defining feature of these temples is their **stellate (star-shaped) ground plan**, formed by multiple projecting angles that transform a simple square plan into a complex, star-like structure. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Madhyamaka and Yogacara schools of Buddhism, consider the following statements:
I. - Madhyamaka is a Hinayana school, whereas Yogacara belongs to the Mahayana tradition.
II. 	 Madhyamaka is closely associated with the doctrine of Sunyata, while Yogacara propounds the doctrine of Vijnaptimatra.
III. 	 Both the schools accept the existence of a permanent self (atman).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Madhyamaka** and **Yogacara** are the two major **philosophical schools** of **Buddhism**. While Madhyamaka emphasises emptiness and a **critical dialectical** method, Yogacara develops an idealist **metaphysics** centred on **consciousness** and its transformation through disciplined practice.
● Both Madhyamaka and Yogacara are schools of **Mahayana Buddhism**. Madhyamaka, systematised by **Nagarjuna**, is not Hīnayana; it belongs to the Mahayana school of Buddhism and represents Mahayana’s critique of all **metaphysical** positions. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Madhyamaka** school is closely associated with the **doctrine of Sunyata.**  It is also known as **Sunyavada.** It teaches that all phenomena are **empty of inherent existence** (svabhava) and uses the doctrine of dependent origination to reject extreme metaphysical views of **eternalism** and **nihilism**. On the other hand, **Yogacara** advances the doctrine of **Vijnaptimatra (“consciousness-only”),** asserting that the empirical world is a projection of **consciousness**. The ultimate reality is pure consciousness, **free from subject–object duality. So, statement II is correct.**
● **Both schools** firmly **reject** the notion of a **permanent self (atman).** Madhyamaka denies any **inherent essence in all entities,** while Yogacara also rejects atman, explaining continuity through evolving streams of **consciousness** instead. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following texts:
I. - Madhyamaka Karika
II. 	 Netti Pakarana
III. 	 Yoga Sutra
IV. 	 Kalpa Sutra
V. 	 Lalitavistara
How many of the above are associated with Buddhism?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Madhyamaka Karika**, authored by **Nagarjuna**, is a foundational **Mahayana Buddhist** **philosophical text** explaining the doctrine of **Shunyata (emptiness)** through dialectical reasoning, rejecting **absolutism** and **nihilism**, and influencing later Buddhist thought. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Netti Pakarana** is a **Theravada Buddhist** text that provides methodological guidelines for interpreting the **Buddha’s teachings,** focusing on hermeneutics, **classification of doctrines**, and analytical tools used in Pali Canon explanation. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Yoga Sutra,** attributed to **Patanjali**, is a classical Indian philosophical text systematizing Yoga philosophy, outlining **ashtanga Yoga** and concepts such as **chitta, sam****ā****dhi, and liberation**, forming a core text of the Yoga school. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Kalpa Sutra** is an important **Jain canonical text** attributed to **Bhadrabahu**, detailing monastic rules, rituals, biographies of **Tirthankaras**, especially Mahavira, and is ritually read during the **Paryushana** **festival**. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Lalitavistara** is a **Mahayana Buddhist text** narrating the life of Gautama Buddha, from his descent from **Tushita** **Heaven** to his first sermon, blending prose and verse, **emphasizing miraculous** **events**, and influencing **Buddhist art** and **iconography**. **So, point V is correct.** **So, only three of the texts given above are associated with Buddhism.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements, with regard to the current global energy scenario:
I. - Fossil fuels account for over 60% of the world’s primary energy mix.
II. 	 In recent years, renewable energy sources have overtaken coal in global electricity generation.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **primary energy mix** includes all energy sources used for electricity, transport, heating, and industrial processes. Fossil fuels still dominate this mix globally.
● As per the United Nations, fossil fuels (comprising **coal, oil, and natural gas**) continue to dominate the **global primary energy mix**, accounting for **over 60% of total energy consumption** **worldwide**. Despite rapid growth in renewables, the overall energy system (including transport, industry, and heating) remains heavily dependent on fossil fuels. **So, statement I is correct**.
● Recent data from global energy think tank Ember indicates a **historic shift in electricity generation**, with **renewable energy sources overtaking coal as the world’s largest source of electricity** in recent years. Strong growth in **solar and wind power** met all incremental global electricity demand and even contributed to a decline in coal-based power generation. **So, statement II is correct**.
○ Solar is the leading source of new capacity additions globally for multiple consecutive years, according to S&P Global. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
A semi-aquatic predator discovered from freshwater streams of the Western Ghats hunts on the water surface by sensing ripples and is considered an indicator of riparian ecosystem health. This newly described species belongs to
which one of the following?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● **The raft spider (Dolomedes indicus)** discovered in the freshwater streams of Wayanad (Western Ghats), is a semi-aquatic spider that belongs to the **genus Dolomedes**.
● It hunts by resting its legs on the water surface to detect vibrations and ripples from prey like small fish and insects. Because it requires clean, unpolluted water to survive, it serves as a biological indicator for riparian ecosystem health. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The discovery of Dolomedes indicus in Wayanad, Kerala, marks the first confirmed record of a raft (fishing) spider from India.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The agriculture sector is the largest contributor to India’s methane emissions.
**Statement II:** Ruminant livestock release methane during digestion. **Statement III:** Rice paddies are significant sources of methane and nitrous oxide.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **a**
**Explanation:**
● **Agriculture** is a major source of methane emissions, particularly in developing countries that generate nearly **three-quarters of direct global greenhouse gases; India,** the **third-largest methane** **emitter**, faces growing pressure to curb agricultural emissions as global methane levels continue to rise.
● In India, Agriculture overall accounts for **around 47–56% of total anthropogenic methane** **emissions**. Within agricultural emissions, **enteric fermentation is the largest source (about**
**54.6%)**, followed by fertilizer use and rice cultivation (about 17.5%). **So, statement I is correct.**
● Ruminant livestock such as cattle, sheep, and goats release methane during digestion due to **microbial** **fermentation under anaerobic conditions in the rumen**. This process produces volatile fatty acids for animal energy, along with gases like methane and carbon dioxide, which are expelled mainly through eructation. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Rice paddies are significant sources of methane and nitrous oxide; however, methane emissions from rice fields arise due to **anaerobic (not aerobic) decomposition of organic matter under flooded** **conditions**, not aerobic decomposition. Flooded paddy fields create oxygen-deficient environments that favour methanogenic bacteria. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText: Which one of the following best describes the term cometabolic bioremediation?

options.a: A process in which microorganisms directly use contaminants as their primary source of energy and carbon for growth
options.b: A method in which pollutants are broken down incidentally by enzymes produced while microbes metabolise another substance
options.c: A technique that removes pollutants by blowing air through contaminated soil or water
options.d: A method that destroys pollutants by adding chemical oxidising agents

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● **Bioremediation** is an eco-friendly and cost-effective technology that uses living organisms, mainly microorganisms such as bacteria, fungi, algae, and sometimes plants, to degrade, detoxify, or immobilize environmental pollutants in soil, water, and air.
● **Cometabolic bioremediation** is a biological remediation approach in which contaminants are degraded **incidentally** by microbial enzymes or cofactors that are produced while microbes metabolise other compounds for their own energy and carbon needs.
● In this process, the contaminant itself does not support microbial growth but is transformed fortuitously during normal microbial metabolism. It allows cometabolic bioremediation to work even at **very low** **contaminant concentrations**, often reducing pollutants to non-detectable levels.
● Cometabolic bioremediation can operate under **aerobic or anaerobic conditions**, though aerobic cometabolism is more commonly applied in practice.
○ Under aerobic conditions, contaminants are typically oxidised by oxygenase enzymes produced during the microbial utilisation of substrates such as methane, propane, ammonia, or toluene. ○ Under anaerobic conditions, contaminants are reduced using enzymes generated during the metabolism of substrates like methanol, acetate, lactate, or sulfate. A suitable substrate is therefore essential to stimulate enzyme production and sustain the cometabolic process. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to uranium contamination:
I. - The widespread use of phosphate-based fertilizers in agriculture can lead to uranium contamination of groundwater.
II. 	 The most severe uranium contamination in India has been reported from the northwestern Indo-Gangetic plains.
III. 	 The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) has not prescribed any specific maximum permissible limit for uranium in drinking water.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Recent assessments by the **Central Groundwater Board (CGWB)** show that **uranium** **contamination in groundwater has been detected in at least 12 Indian states**, with the **north-** **western Indo-Gangetic plains emerging as the most severely affected region**.
○ Uranium is a **nephrotoxic element**. Multiple studies, including those cited by CGWB, Panjab University and Duke University, have established that **long-term consumption of uranium-** **contaminated groundwater increases the risk of impaired renal function and kidney** **disease**, and may also cause bone toxicity.
● While the **primary source of uranium is geogenic**, studies highlight that anthropogenic factors intensify uranium mobilisation, including **over-extraction of groundwater, nitrate pollution, and** **agricultural practices**. The use of **phosphate-based fertilizers**, which often contain uranium as an impurity, contributes indirectly by increasing nitrate levels and altering groundwater chemistry, thereby facilitating uranium release into groundwater. **So, statement I is correct**.
● The most severe uranium contamination in India's groundwater is indeed reported from the IndoGangetic plains. States such as **Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh and Delhi** form a major uranium hotspot. **So, statement II is correct**.
● The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, in line with the **World Health Organization (WHO)**, prescribes a **maximum permissible limit of 30 µg/L (ppb)** for uranium in drinking water. **So,** **statement III is not correct**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Central Ground Water Board (CGWB) and Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) reports highlight severe heavy metal contamination in Delhi’s groundwater.

questions.subject: Science & Technology

questions.questionText:
With reference to Chlorpyrifos, recently seen in the news, consider the following statements:
I. - It is a biological pesticide derived from plantbased compounds.
II. 	 It can disrupt nerve signal transmission, leading to paralysis.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Chlorpyrifos** is a **synthetic organophosphate insecticide** widely used on crops such as rice, cotton, sugarcane, and vegetables, as well as for controlling urban pests and termites. It is **chemically** **manufactured, not plant-derived**, and acts by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, disrupting nerve signal transmission. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Chlorpyrifos s mode of action involve**s interference with the nervous system**, causing neurological symptoms such as **muscle tremors, dizziness, and paralysis.** Its persistence and bioaccumulation make it a concern for both ecosystems and human health, as residues can remain in agricultural produce and the environment for long periods.   **So, statement II is correct.**
● Because of these risks, chlorpyrifos has been proposed for regulation under the **Stockholm** **Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs).** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, India has opposed the inclusion of Chlorpyrifos in the Stockholm lists of prohibited chemicals

questions.questionText:
Which of the following are the promising geothermal sites for electric power generation in India?
I. - Puga Valley in Jammu and Kashmir
II. 	 Khammam in Telangana
III. 	 Surajkund in Jharkhand
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
As on date, around 77 GW of renewable energy capacity has been installed in the country with major share coming from solar and wind power technologies. The possibility of venturing into new emerging renewable energy technologies, such as Floating Solar, Offshore wind, solar wind hybrid, energy storage, etc is also being explored. However, **renewable energy technologies such as geo-thermal and ocean energy still** **remain at a nascent stage in India.**
● **Geo-thermal:** As per preliminary investigations undertaken by the GSI, there are around 300 geothermal hot springs in India. Most of these geothermal hot springs are in medium potential (100 C to 200 C) and low potential (<100 C) zones. The promising geothermal sites for electric power generation are **Puga Valley & Chummathang in Jammu & Kashmir**, Cambay in Gujarat, Tattapani in Chattisgarh, **Khammam in Telangana** & Ratnagiri in Maharashtra. The promising geothermal sites for direct heat use applications are Rajgir in Bihar, Manikaran in Himachal Pradesh, **Surajkund in** **Jharkhand**, Tapoban in Uttarakhand & Sohana region in Haryana. **So, points 1, 2 and 3 are correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following description:
I. - These forests occur in regions receiving rainfall of about 70–100 cm.
II. 	 These forests are highly susceptible to forest fires.
III. 	 These forests do not have a constant, yearround decomposition of leaf litter.
Which of the following forest types is being described above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Tropical dry deciduous forests** are one of the **most** **widespread forest types in India**. They occur in regions receiving **70–100 cm of annual rainfall**.
● Trees in these forests **shed their leaves for about 6–8 weeks** **during the dry summer,** which helps reduce water loss through transpiration. During the dry season, these forests experience **frequent surface fires.** These fires are a key ecological driver that influences species composition by favouring fire-resistant or fire-adapted species.
● The prolonged dry period also means that **leaf litter does not** **decompose continuously throughout the year**, unlike in tropical evergreen forests, where warmth and moisture persist year-round. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Based on **rainfall and water availability**, they are divided into **moist** and **dry** **deciduous forests**.
● **Moist deciduous forests** are found in areas with **100–200** **cm rainfall**, mainly in the **northeastern states**, the **foothills of the Himalayas**, **Jharkhand**, **west Odisha**, **Chhattisgarh**, and the **eastern slopes of the Western Ghats**.
● **Dry deciduous forests** occur in regions receiving **70–100 cm rainfall**, chiefly in the **rainier parts of the Peninsular Plateau** and the **plains of Bihar and Uttar** **Pradesh**.
● Examples of trees in these forests are **Teak** **sal, bamboo, shisham, sandalwood,** **khair, kusum, arjun, mulberry etc.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Mexico is the world’s leading producer of silver.
**Statement II:** Mexico holds the largest silver reserves in the world.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation for Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II is not the correct explanation for Statement I.
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mexico** is the **world’s largest producer of silver**, supported by a long-established mining sector, high-grade ore bodies, and strong integration with global bullion and industrial markets. **So,  statement** **I is correct**.
● **Mexico does not possess the largest silver reserves**. According to recent global reserve estimates, the **largest silver reserves are held by Peru**, followed by **Australia**, **Russia**, **China**, and **Poland**. **So,  statement II is not correct**.
● Silver is both a **precious metal** and a critical **industrial input**, widely used in electronics, solar panels, medical devices, and as a store of value.
● Other major silver-producing countries include **Bolivia, Poland, Chile, Russia,** the United **States,** **Australia, Kazakhstan, Argentina** and **India.**
● Despite being a significant producer, **India is a major importer** due to strong investment demand. **So, Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Minerals and Resources (Coal, Iron, Bauxite, Lead, Zinc, Pyrites, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are the reasons for Greenland being rich in natural resources?
I. - Mountain-building processes create faults and fractures that concentrate minerals
II. 	 The rifting associated with the opening of the Atlantic Ocean created sedimentary basins that host natural resources
III. 	 Long-term volcanic activity leading to deposition of rare-earth elements
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Greenland** is one of the most **resource-rich** regions in the **Arctic** due to its unique geological history spanning nearly **4 billion years.** Its mineral deposits and energy reserves are linked to **repeated** **tectonic activity,** rifting events, and volcanism that shaped its crust and concentrated valuable materials.
● **Mountain-building** created **faults** and **fractures** in Greenland’s rocks. These cracks acted as channels for **mineral-rich fluids,** which deposited valuable resources like **gold, diamonds, rubies, and** **graphite** over long geological periods. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Rifting,** linked to the opening of the **Atlantic Ocean,** around **200 million years ago, formed** deep **sedimentary** basins. Such basins are ideal for the **accumulation** and **storage** of **oil and natural gas,** making Greenland rich in **hydrocarbons**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Repeated volcanic episodes** deposited rare minerals and metals. These processes helped concentrate **rare-earth elements (REEs),** which are essential for electronics, **renewable** energy technology, **batteries**, and **defence** equipment, increasing Greenland’s strategic value. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Greenland is currently in the spotlight as a massive, largely untapped repository of rare earth elements (REEs), including dysprosium and neodymium, crucial for renewable energy and high-tech industries.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
With reference to climatology, desertification can
lead to a “localized cooling” effect that further
reduces rainfall. This is primarily due to:

options.a:
An increase in surface albedo leading to
higher reflection of solar radiation
options.c:
Increased cloud condensation nuclei from
dust aerosols

questions.explanationMarkdown: ● **Desertification** can lead to a **localized cooling effect** that further reduces rainfall, primarily due to changes in **surface albedo**. When vegetation is lost and land becomes bare or sandy, the surface reflects more solar radiation instead of absorbing it. This reduces the heating of the land surface, weakening local convection currents and cloud formation, which in turn decreases rainfall. The process creates a **feedback loop**, where reduced precipitation accelerates aridity and further desertification. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText:
Consider the following phenomena:
I. - Orogeny
II. 	 Geomagnetic reversal
III. 	 Volcanism
IV. 	 Glacial cycles
How many of the above have influenced the
evolution, dispersal, and extinction of organisms
in the history of Earth?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Throughout Earth’s history, large-scale geological and climatic processes have played a decisive role in shaping the evolution, dispersal, and extinction of living organisms.
● **Orogeny (Mountain Building):** It creates major physical barriers that isolate populations, leading to allopatric speciation. It also alters regional and global climates by influencing wind patterns and creating rain shadows, forcing organisms to adapt or face extinction. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Geomagnetic Reversal:** Its effect on evolution is **indirect and less pronounced** than other processes. Magnetic field weakening may increase exposure to cosmic/solar radiation, potentially raising mutation rates, but evidence linking it directly to major extinctions is limited. It can, however, influence evolutionary transitions over long timescales. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Volcanism:** Large-scale volcanic events release greenhouse gases and aerosols, triggering rapid climate changes, acid rain, and habitat loss, **which have been linked to several mass extinctions in Earth’s** **history.** For example, the **Siberian Traps volcanism** is the primary suspect for the Permian-Triassic extinction, which **wiped out roughly 95% of marine species. So, point III is correct.**
● **Glacial Cycles:** Glaciations and interglacial periods cause temperature and sea-level fluctuations. These cycles cause major fluctuations in temperature and sea level. Glaciations create land bridges that facilitate species dispersal, while interglacial periods isolate populations, promoting speciation and extinction. **So,** **point IV is correct.** **So, all of the above have influenced the evolution, dispersal, and extinction of organisms in the** **history of Earth.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History

questions.questionText:
Somaliland, often seen in the news, historically
refers to a region that largely comprises parts of
which of the following present-day areas?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Somaliland** historically refers to the region that largely comprises present-day **Somalia and Djibouti.** During the colonial era, it was a British protectorate known as British Somaliland. It gained brief independence in June 1960 before uniting with Italian Somaliland to form the modern state of Somalia. The region has a distinct colonial and cultural history, which has shaped its unique identity compared to other parts of Somalia.
● Following the collapse of Somalia’s central government in 1991, Somaliland declared independence and established its own government, constitution, currency, and security forces, with **Hargeisa as its capital.** Although it functions as a de facto state, it remains internationally unrecognized. In recent years, Somaliland has gained attention for its pursuit of formal recognition and its strategic role in the Horn of Africa, including security arrangements, trade initiatives, and regional diplomacy. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Israel formally recognised the self-declared Republic of Somaliland as an independent and sovereign state.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the removal of a Judge of the
Supreme Court or a High Court in India, consider
the following statements:
I. - The Constitution of India allows the removal
of a judge solely on the grounds of "proved
misbehaviour or incapacity".
II. 	 The motion to remove a judge must be
supported by 100 Lok Sabha members or 50
Rajya Sabha members for its introduction.
III. 	 While admitting or refusing to admit a
removal motion, the Speaker or the Chairman
acts as a statutory authority.
IV. 	 No Judge of the Supreme Court or a High
Court has been removed from the office
through the impeachment process in India.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constitution of India** explicitly provides that a Judge can be removed **only on the grounds of** **“proved misbehaviour or incapacity”**. This is laid down under **Article 124(4)** for Supreme Court Judges and **Article 217(1)(b)** for High Court Judges. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968,** prescribes the procedural framework for removal. It mandates that a **removal motion must be signed by at least 100 members of the Lok Sabha or 50 members** **of the Rajya Sabha** before being submitted to the Speaker or Chairman, respectively. **So, statement** **II is correct**.
● Under the above-mentioned Act, while deciding whether to **admit or disallow the motion**, the **Speaker or Chairman exercises powers as a statutory authority** derived from legislation, and not merely as the Presiding Officer of the House. This role is distinct from routine parliamentary functions. **So, statement III is correct**.
○ According to the Supreme Court of India in the landmark Sub-Committee on Judicial Accountability v. Union of India (1991) case, when the Speaker of the Lok Sabha or the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha decides whether to admit or refuse a removal motion for a judge, they act as a statutory authority under the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968.
● India has **not witnessed the successful impeachment of any Supreme Court or High Court** **Judge so far**. The notable case of **Justice V. Ramaswami (1991–93)** demonstrates this. Although the Inquiry Committee found him guilty of misbehaviour, the impeachment motion failed in the Lok Sabha due to a lack of the required special majority. **So,** **statement IV is correct**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts on Tenure and Removal of Judges in India:**
● Judges of the **Supreme Court** retire at the age of **65 years**, while **High Court Judges** retire at **62 years**.
● A Judge may **resign voluntarily** by writing to the **President of India**.
● **Impeachment terminology is not used in the Constitution** for judges; it is formally applicable only to the **President of India**.
● Article 124(5) empowers Parliament to make a law regulating the **procedure for investigation** **and proof** of misbehaviour or incapacity. Pursuant to this power, Parliament enacted the **Judges** **(Inquiry) Act, 1968**.
● Article 124(4) lays down the **substantive requirement** for removal: a judge can be removed only by an address passed by **each House of Parliament**, supported by a **majority of the** **total membership of that House** and by a **two-thirds majority of members present and** **voting**, followed by an order of removal by the President.
**Relevance:** In December 2025, 107 Members of Parliament in the Lok Sabha submitted a notice seeking the removal of Justice G.R. Swaminathan.

questions.subject: International Relations

questions.questionText:
Which of the following countries are **not** members
of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization
(NATO)?
I. - United Kingdom
II. 	 Sweden
III. 	 Belarus
IV. 	 Hungary
V. 	 Bulgaria
VI. 	Finland
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:

options.b: I, III, IV and VI only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **North Atlantic Treaty Organization(NATO)** was formed in 1949 with the signing of the Washington Treaty. **NATO is a security alliance of 31 countries from North America and** **Europe**. NATO’s fundamental goal is to **safeguard the Allies’ freedom and security by political** **and military means**. NATO remains the principal security instrument of the transatlantic community and the expression of its common democratic values.
● NATO was set up after World War II with the express purpose of containing the Soviet Union. The **members of the alliance include**: since 1949, the 12 founding countries of Belgium, Canada, Denmark, France, Iceland, Italy, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, the **United** **Kingdom,** and the United States; Greece and Turkey joined in 1952; Germany in 1955; Spain in 1982; Czechia, **Hungary,** and Poland in 1999; **Bulgaria,** Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, Slovakia, and Slovenia joined in 2004; Albania and Croatia in 2009; Montenegro in 2017; North Macedonia in 2020; and finally, **Finland.**  At present, NATO has 31 member countries. **So, points I, IV, V and VI are** **correct.**
● **Sweden and Belarus are not members of the NATO alliance. So, points II and III are not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** An accused person can refuse to provide DNA, blood samples, or handwriting specimens for the purpose of investigation.
**Statement II:** Article 20(3) of the Constitution of India prohibits compelling an accused person to be a witness against himself.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Right against self-incrimination under Article 20(3)** protects an accused only from **testimonial compulsion,** that is, being forced to convey personal knowledge through oral or written statements. As clarified by constitutional and judicial interpretation, this protection **does not extend** **to compulsory production of material or physical evidence**, such as **DNA samples, blood** **samples, handwriting or signature specimens, fingerprints, or compulsory exhibition of the** **body**. Therefore, **refusal to provide such samples is not protected** under Article 20(3). **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
○ In **State of Bombay v. Kathi Kalu Oghad (1961)**, the Supreme Court clarified that the prohibition under Article 20(3) applies only to testimonial compulsion and not to the collection of physical evidence. This position was reaffirmed in **Selvi v. State of Karnataka (2010)**.
● **Article 20(3)** indeed prohibits compelling an accused to **be a witness against himself**, i.e., to give **testimonial evidence** that may be self-incriminatory. The scope of this protection is limited to **criminal proceedings** and to **testimonial evidence**, not to physical or forensic evidence. **So,** **statement II is correct.** **So, Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following parliamentary committees:
I. - Committee on Public Accounts
II. 	 Committee on Estimates
III. 	 Committee on Public Undertakings
IV. 	 Committee on Government Assurances
How many of the above oversee financial discipline and propriety in governance?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Parliamentary Committees** are bodies of Members of Parliament constituted by either House or by the Presiding Officers to assist Parliament in its functions. They operate under the supervision of the Speaker or Chairman, submit their reports to the House, and are rooted in British parliamentary tradition. Their constitutional basis is derived primarily from Articles 105 and 118, which relate to parliamentary privileges and the regulation of legislative procedure.
● The **Public Accounts Committee (PAC)** plays a central role in enforcing probity by examining the **audit reports of the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG)**. It evaluates public expenditure not only from the legal and formal perspective but also from the standpoint of **economy, prudence,** **propriety, and wisdom**, thereby exposing cases of **waste, loss, corruption, extravagance, and** **inefficiency. So, point I is correct**.
● The **Estimates Committee** examines budget estimates with the objective of suggesting **economies,** **efficiency, and administrative reforms** consistent with government policy. By assessing whether public money is **well laid out** and suggesting improvements in organisation and administration, it contributes indirectly but significantly to **ethical governance and financial discipline**. **So, point II** **is correct**.
● The **Committee on Public Undertakings (COPU)** ensures **financial propriety** within public sector enterprises by examining their reports and accounts to verify if they are being managed according to sound business principles and prudent commercial practices. **So, point III is correct**.
● **The Committee on Government Assurances** is **not a financial committee**. Its specific mandate is to track and report on the extent to which promises or undertakings made by Ministers on the floor of the House have been implemented. While it ensures accountability, it does not have a primary role in enforcing financial discipline or auditing expenditure. **So, point IV is not correct** **So, only three of the above committees enable Parliament to ensure financial discipline and** **propriety in governance.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Parliamentary Committees:**
● The **Public Accounts Committee** is regarded as the most powerful financial committee of Parliament and works closely with the **Comptroller and Auditor** **General (CAG)**. A long-standing convention ensures that its **Chairperson is from** **the Opposition**, reinforcing non-partisan scrutiny and democratic accountability.
● The **Estimates Committee** is unique in being composed **only of Lok Sabha** **members** and is often described as a **“continuous economy committee”**, as it functions throughout the financial year to suggest systemic improvements in administrative functioning and expenditure management.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The President of India addresses the first sitting of all sessions of every fiscal year.
II. 	 Notices of Amendments to the Motion of Thanks can be given only after the President has delivered the Address.
III. 	 Non-passage of the Motion of Thanks in the Parliament leads to the defeat of the Government.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The first session after each general election** and the **first session of every fiscal year** is addressed by the **President of India**. In this address, the President outlines the policies and programmes of the Government in the preceding year and ensuing year. The **address is followed by** **a motion of thanks** moved in each House by ruling party MPs, and this is called a ‘Motion of Thanks’
which must be passed, otherwise, **it amounts to the defeat of the Government. So, statement** **I is not correct and statement III is correct.**
● **Notices of amendments to the Motion of Thanks can be given only after the President has** **delivered the Address.** Lists of amendments are, however, circulated to members only after the notice of Motion of Thanks is received and published in the Bulletin. As a matter of convention, amendments to the Motion of Thanks are given only by members belonging to a party in opposition to the Government. In 1991, however, some members belonging to the ruling party also gave notices of and moved the amendments to the Motion of Thanks. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● In the case of the first session after each general election to the Lok Sabha, the President addresses both Houses of Parliament assembled together after the members of the Lok Sabha have made and subscribed to the oath or affirmation and the Speaker has been elected by the Lok Sabha. The President of India has addressed both Houses of Parliament assembled together **twice in the years 1957, 1962, 1989, 1991, 1996** **and in 2009** when **general elections also took place** after the first session of the year had been held.
