questions.questionText:
With reference to the Bar Council of India (BCI),
consider the following statements:
I. - It is a statutory body established by Parliament
under the Advocates Act, 1961.
II. 	 The Attorney General of India and the
Solicitor General of India are its ex officio
members.
III. 	 It is responsible for conducting judicial
service examinations for the recruitment of
judges.
IV. 	 It does not recognise foreign law qualifications
for the enrolment of advocates in India.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Bar Council of India (BCI)** is a **statutory body** created by **Parliament under the Advocates** **Act, 1961,** to regulate the legal profession and legal education in India. **So, statement I is correct**.
● As a **regulatory authority**, the Bar Council of India prescribes **standards of professional conduct** **and etiquette** for advocates and exercises **disciplinary jurisdiction** through its disciplinary committees. It lays down the **procedures to be followed by disciplinary committees** at both the central and State Bar Council levels, thereby ensuring accountability and ethical behaviour within the legal profession.
● The **Attorney-General of India** and the **Solicitor-General of India** are members **ex officio**, signifying the institutional connection between the highest law officers of the Union and the regulatory body of advocates. In addition, **each State Bar Council elects one of its members** to represent it in the Bar Council of India, ensuring that the concerns and perspectives of advocates across states are institutionally represented at the national level. **So, statement II is correct**.
● The BCI is **not responsible for conducting judicial service examinations**. These exams for the recruitment of judges are typically conducted by State Public Service Commissions and High Courts. The BCI’s primary examination is the All India Bar Examination (AIBE) for law graduates seeking to practice as advocates.  **So, statement III is not correct**.
● BCI under **Section 7 of the Advocates Act, 1961,** is responsible for promoting **legal education**, **laying down standards of legal education**, and **recognising universities whose law degrees** **qualify a person for enrolment as an advocate**.
● It recognises **foreign law qualifications on a reciprocal basis**, enabling qualified foreign-trained lawyers to seek enrolment in India, subject to prescribed conditions. **So, statement IV is not correct**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act, 1967:
I. - It classifies acts intended to cause ‘disaffection
against India’ as ‘terrorist acts’.
II. 	 It extends criminal liability for terrorist
activities to companies, trusts and societies.
III. 	 It empowers only police officers of the rank
of Deputy Superintendent of Police or above
to investigate offences under it.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act** **(UAPA), 1967,** was enacted to address **anti-national** **activities and secessionist movements** in India.
● **Major provisions of the UAPA, 1967:** ○ Any **act, speech, writing, or visual representation** by **individuals or groups** intended to cause **“disaffection against India”** is legally classified as **“unlawful activity”, not a** **“terrorist act”**. **So, statement I is not correct.** ○ The **2012 amendment** to the UAPA (1967) expanded **criminal liability** for terrorism to include **companies, societies, and trusts**, ensuring institutional entities are held accountable for **facilitating or engaging in terror activities**. **So, statement II is correct.** ○ Originally, only **Deputy Superintendents or Assistant Commissioners** could investigate cases. The **2019 amendment** expanded this power, authorizing **National Investigation** **Agency (NIA) officers of Inspector rank or higher** to conduct investigations. **So,** **statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Supreme Court has denied bail to Delhi riot's accused based on an expansive statutory definition of a ‘terrorist act’.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to National Intelligence Grid (NATGRID):
I. - It is a statutory body.
II. 	 It is exempt from the purview of the Right to Information Act, 2005, subject to certain exceptions.
III. 	 State police officers of the rank of Superintendent of Police and above are authorised to access its data.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **National Intelligence Grid (NATGRID)** provides Indian law enforcement with **real-time**, secure access to various **government and private** databases. It centralizes data on **terrorism and** **economic crimes** to strengthen national security capabilities.
● **NATGRID** is an **“attached office”** under the **Ministry of Home Affairs**. It was not created by an Act of Parliament (which is required for statutory status). **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **NATGRID** was conceptualized in **2009** following the **26/11 Mumbai terror attacks** **(not the 2002** **Parliament attack)** to address the lack of real-time information sharing between agencies.
● **NATGRID** has been included in the **Second Schedule** of the RTI Act, 2005, which **exempts it from** **the law's disclosure requirements** (except for cases involving corruption or human rights violations). **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **National Population Register (NPR)** is one of the 21 categories of data **linked to the** **NATGRID** platform, providing family-wise details and household linkages.
● Officers of the rank of **Superintendent of Police (SP) and above** can access the database through nodal agencies to track suspects and perform data analysis. **So, statement III is correct**. **So, only two of the statements given above are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, officials said that the National Intelligence Grid (NATGRID) is slowly gathering pace and receiving 45k requests a month.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Financial Markets
questions.questionText:
With reference to electoral funding in India, consider the following statements:
I. - A company should be at least three years old to make a political contribution.
II. 	 The cash donation limit for tax exemption purposes is ₹20,000.
III. 	 The Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999, primarily governs the receipt of funds by political parties from foreign sources.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Electoral funding in India** involves financial contributions from **individuals, organizations, or** **political parties** to support election campaigns. Key mechanisms include **individual donations, state** **funding, corporate contributions, electoral trusts, and electoral bonds**. These fundings are regulated by laws like the **Representation of the People Act, 1951, and the Income Tax Act,** **1961**.
● Under the **Companies Act, 2013**, a company must generally be in existence for **three years** before it can donate to political parties; government companies and newer companies are barred. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The **Finance Act 2017** reduced the cash donation threshold for political parties radically - **from** **₹****20,000 to** **₹****2,000** (as per the Finance Minister’s 2017 budget announcement and subsequent amendment). That means **cash donations of more than** **₹****2,000 must be made via banking** **channels** to claim income-tax exemptions. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **The Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999, does not primarily govern foreign** **contributions to Indian political parties**. Instead, **Section 3 of the Foreign Contribution** **(Regulation) Act, 2010 (FCRA)** is the **principal statute** that regulates (and largely prohibits) foreign funding to political entities. **So, statement IIII is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Point to Remember**
● Indian election law (Representation of the People Act and conduct rules) prescribes **expenditure limits only for individual candidates, not political parties**. Parties can spend unlimited amounts on election campaigns.
**Relevance:** The Indian Supreme Court handed down a landmark decision on February 15th, 2024, striking down the controversial Electoral Bond scheme as unconstitutional.

questions.subject: Science & Technology

questions.questionText: The ‘Weimar Triangle’, recently seen in the news, is best described as a:

options.a: Trilateral forum comprising France, Germany and Poland to coordinate positions on European security and foreign policy
options.b: Regional trade bloc among Germany, Austria and Switzerland for economic integration
options.c: Post-Cold War military alliance between the United Kingdom, France and Germany
options.d: Clean energy partnership among the Baltic States and Poland to promote renewable energy cooperation

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● The Weimar Triangle is a **trilateral political** and **diplomatic cooperation framework** **established** **in 1991** in Weimar, Germany, **by France, Germany, and Poland.** It aims to foster reconciliation, deepen political dialogue, and support European integration. It serves as a vital forum for security and economic cooperation, particularly for addressing regional challenges and strengthening the EU.
● Greece, Italy, and Austria are members of the European Union, but they are not part of the Weimar Triangle grouping. **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The External Affairs Minister of India participated in India’s first-ever engagement in the Weimar Triangle, with French and Polish counterparts, along with German representatives.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following aspects signify similarities
between India's foreign policy and Japan’s Sushi
Diplomacy?
I. - Buddhist Diplomacy
II. 	 Yoga Diplomacy
III. 	 Chequebook Diplomacy
IV. 	 Vaccine Maitri
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Japan’s Sushi Diplomacy** refers to the use of **cultural symbols** and **everyday consumption** **practices** such as food, cuisine, and popular culture to signal **political solidarity, build goodwill,** and counter strategic pressure without overt coercion. It is a form of **soft power diplomacy.**
● India uses shared **Buddhist heritage**, Buddhist sites, **pilgrimages**, monks’ exchanges, and cultural festivals to deepen ties with **East and Southeast Asian countries.** Like Sushi Diplomacy, it leverages **civilizational and cultural affinity** to strengthen strategic relationships. **So, point I is correct.**
● Through initiatives like the **International Day of Yoga,** India promotes yoga as a cultural and civilizational practice. This enhances **India’s global image, people-to-people ties,** and normative influence, **closely mirroring** the symbolic, non-coercive outreach seen in Japan’s Sushi Diplomacy. **So,** **point II is correct.**
● **Chequebook diplomacy** relies on **direct financial aid** or investment to gain political influence, often **transactional in nature.** Sushi Diplomacy, by **contrast**, is cultural and symbolic rather than monetary or **quid pro quo. So, point III is not correct.**
● **India’s Vaccine Maitri** involved supplying vaccines to partner countries to build goodwill and **trust** **during a crisis.** While humanitarian in delivery, it functioned as **soft power** outreach, similar in spirit to Japan’s use of food and **cultural gestures** to convey solidarity. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Taiwanese President recently utilized sushi diplomacy, posting images eating Japanese seafood to show solidarity with Tokyo against rising Chinese pressure.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following countries recently
launched a 'Gold Card' programme that provides
a fast-track pathway to permanent residency and
citizenship?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Trump Gold Card** is a new **fast-track residency programme** launched by the **United States** **of America** in 2025. Unlike previous investor visas (like the EB-5), this program requires a direct, nonrefundable "gift" to the U.S. Treasury rather than a business investment.
● Individuals must pay at least **USD 1 million,** while businesses sponsoring employees must pay **USD 2** **million.** A proposed **“Platinum”** version costing USD 5 million offers tax benefits. Applicants also pay a non-refundable **USD 15,000 processing fee.**
● The scheme is positioned as an **alternative to traditional green cards**, aimed at attracting high-networth individuals and **skilled professionals,** while the US simultaneously **tightens regular** **immigration** and asylum policies. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The US President has recently launched the Gold Card visa programme, inviting individuals and companies to invest at least $1 million.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Region in the news Country
l. Borno and Adamawa Nigeria
II. - Daraa and Suwayda Jordan
III. - Senkaku Islands Russia
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Borno** and **Adamawa** are northeastern states of **Nigeria**. They have been epicentres of the **Boko Haram** and **ISWaP** insurgency since 2009, involving attacks on **civilians, security forces**, and **infrastructure**. The violence has caused mass displacement, humanitarian crises, and **cross-border insecurity** affecting the Lake Chad Basin. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Daraa** and **Suwayda** are southern **Syrian** governorates that have recently been in the news due to **Israeli** **airstrikes** on Syrian military positions and Iran-linked targets in southern Syria. Suwayda is also significant due to unrest among the **Druze minority,** driven by **economic** **collapse,** forced recruitment, and opposition to the Assad regime.  **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● The **Senkaku Islands** are a group of uninhabited islets in the **East China Sea** administered by **Japan** and claimed by **China** (which calls them Diaoyu). The **Russia–Japan** territorial dispute concerns the **Kuril Islands**/Northern Territories, not the Senkaku Islands.  **So, pair III is not** **correctly matched.** **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, a China Coast Guard ship formation passed through the disputed waters of the Senkaku Islands.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Abraham Accords, consider the following statements:
I. - They were the first-ever peace agreements signed between Israel and Arab countries.
II. 	 They were actively mediated by the United States.
III. 	 Kazakhstan is the newest country to have formally joined the accords.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Abraham Accords** are **normalization** agreements initiated in **2020** to establish diplomatic, economic, and strategic relations between **Israel and several Muslim-majority countries**, reflecting a major realignment in **West Asian diplomacy.**
● The **first peace treaty** between Israel and an Arab nation was signed with **Egypt**, known as the **Camp** **David Accords, in 1979**. The **second** peace treaty was signed with **Jordan** in 1994. So, the **Abraham** **Accords (2020)** are **not the first-ever** peace agreements between Israel and Arab countries. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● The Abraham Accords were **brokered** with active, central **mediation** by the **United States of** **America (USA)**. The USA acted as the primary mediator and facilitator, offering diplomatic and **strategic incentives** to the signatory nations. The initial agreements in September 2020 involved Israel,
the **United Arab Emirates (UaE)**, and **Bahrain**, followed later by **Sudan** and **Morocco**. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● In **November 2025**, **Kazakhstan** formally joined the Abraham Accords, making it the **newest** and **first Central Asian, post-Soviet nation** to join the 2020 initiative normalizing relations between Israel and Muslim-majority nations. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In November 2025, U.S. President announced that Kazakhstan formally joined the Abraham Accords.

questions.questionText: Who among the following was recently awarded the Indira Gandhi Prize for Peace, Disarmament and Development for the year 2025?

options.d: Ali Abu Awwad and Daniel Barenboim

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Indira Gandhi Prize for Peace, Disarmament and Development** was instituted in **1986** by the Indira Gandhi **Memorial Trust** in memory of former Prime Minister Indira Gandhi.
● It is awarded to **individuals or organisations** for outstanding contributions towards international **peace, nuclear and conventional disarmament**, **sustainable development**, and the promotion of a **just international economic order.**
● The award carries a **cash prize of** **₹****25 lakh** along with a citation. It also recognises efforts to ensure that **scientific and technological advances** enhance human freedom and global equity.
● Recently, **Mozambican rights activist** and humanitarian **Graca Machel** has been awarded the Indira Gandhi Prize for Peace, Disarmament and Development for 2025. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Adjuvants:
I. - They help to improve immune activation and reduce antigen dose requirements.
II. 	 All vaccines approved for use in India invariably contain them.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Adjuvants** are important components in **modern vaccine technology**. They help improve **immune** **activation**, reduce antigen dose requirements, and support stronger **long-term immunity**, especially in subunit and **peptide-based vaccines.**
● An adjuvant is an **immune-stimulating substance** added to vaccines to boost the **body’s immune** **response** against a specific **antigen**. **Examples** include **alum (aluminium salts)**. **So, statement I** **is correct.**
● **Not all vaccines require adjuvants.** Live attenuated vaccines (like **measles** or **BCG**) and many **viral-vector vaccines** can generate strong immune responses naturally. So, several vaccines approved in India **may not contain** added adjuvants. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI) and the UK’s Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency launched a global adjuvant library.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText: An employee of an X company is working from home and needs access to the company's sensitive information from his network. He will be given access to the company's network using this technology from his home computer as if he was physically present in the office. In the context of emerging communication technologies, which one of the following terms best applies to the above scenario?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **A VPN (Virtual Private Network)** is a service that creates a safe, encrypted online connection. Internet users may use a VPN to give themselves more privacy and anonymity online or circumvent geographic-based blocking and censorship. A VPN uses tunnelling protocols to encrypt data at the sending end and decrypts it at the receiving end. The originating and receiving network addresses are also encrypted to provide better security for online activities.
● One benefit of a VPN is its data encryption features. Encryption, or putting data into a coded format so its meaning is obscured, allows you to keep confidential information safe. By using VPN, **an individual** **can connect to a company's network** and look at **sensitive materials on their own devices** while away from the office. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Agni-V missile, consider the following statements:
I. - It is a cruise missile.
II. 	 It is equipped with nuclear and Multiple Independently Targetable Reentry Vehicle (MIRV) capability.
III. 	 It is a fire-and-forget missile system.
Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Agni-V** is an Indian **intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM)** with a **range of over 5,000** **km.** It is an **evolution of the Agni-III IRBM**, featuring similar first- and second-stage motors with an added third stage. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The Agni-V is a **nuclear-capable** **land-based** Intercontinental Ballistic Missile (ICBM) with **Multiple** **Independently Targetable Reentry Vehicle (MIRV)** capability. The Agni-V is a **three-stage** **missile system.** All three stages use solid fuel propellant. **Agni-II, Agni-III, and Agni-IV** are examples of **two-stage** solid-fuelled missiles. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Agni-V is a **fire-and-forget** missile, which **once fired cannot be stopped**, except by an interceptor missile. It uses a Ring Laser **Gyroscope-based Inertial Navigation System (RLG-INS),** which is the **core system** responsible for keeping the missile on its programmed path. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
A **Multiple Independently Targetable Reentry Vehicle (MIRV)** is a sophisticated ballistic missile payload system that enables a single missile to deploy several nuclear warheads, each programmed to hit separate targets. This capability enhances strike efficiency and makes missile defense interception far more challenging.
**Relevance:** India successfully test-fired its intermediate-range ballistic missile Agni-5 from the Integrated Test Range at Chandipur, Odisha.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Seaweed Derivative Common Application
I. - Emulsifiers Binding agents in toothpaste
II. - Emollients Softeners in skin care
III. - Biopolymer Fuel additives in Automobiles
How many of the pairs given above are **not** correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Seaweed** is the common name for countless species of marine plants and algae that grow in the ocean as well as in rivers, lakes and other water bodies. Some seaweeds are microscopic, such as the phytoplankton that live suspended in the water column and provide the base for most marine food chains. Some are enormous, like the giant kelp that grows in abundant “forests” and towers like underwater redwoods from its roots at the bottom of the sea.
● Seaweed-derived substances such as **alginates, carrageenan, and agar** function as effective **emulsifiers** and stabilizers. In toothpaste formulations, they act as **binding agents**, ensuring uniform mixing of liquid and solid components, maintaining consistency, and preventing phase separation or drying. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● Seaweed extracts are widely used in cosmetics and personal care products as **emollients**. They help retain moisture, improve skin hydration, and impart a soft and smooth texture, making them common ingredients in skin-care formulations. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● Although seaweed biomass can be converted into biofuels such as bioethanol, **seaweed-derived** **biopolymers** are mainly used for **biodegradable packaging and biopolymeric films**. They are **not** **commonly used as fuel additives in automobiles** in standard industrial practice. **So, pair III is not** **correctly matched.** **So, only one of the pairs given above is not correctly matched.**
**Points to remember**
● **More than 95%** of the seaweed harvested for human consumption is cultivated rather than taken from the wild.
● Unlike land-based crops, **seaweed doesn’t require fertilizer, pesticides,** **or freshwater or land.**
**Therefore, option (a) is the answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText: The term “quasi-moon” refers to which one of the following?

options.a: A small natural satellite that has been permanently captured by Earth's magnetic field
options.b: A fragment of the Moon that has broken off and entered a stable orbit between Earth and Mars
options.c: A near-Earth asteroid that orbits Earth in a highly elliptical orbit beyond the Moon
options.d: An asteroid that orbits the Sun but stays close to Earth, appearing to orbit it due to shared orbital speed

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **quasi-moon** is a rare type of **near-Earth object**, usually an **asteroid**, that **orbits the Sun** but has an **orbital period** very close to Earth’s (about one year). Because it moves in a **1:1 co-orbital** **resonance** with Earth, it appears from our planet’s perspective to loop around Earth in a **figure-eight** **or horseshoe-shaped path**, creating the illusion of being a **temporary moon.**
● However, unlike Earth’s Moon, a quasi-moon is **not gravitationally bound** in a stable Earth-centric orbit and **does not revolve** within the Moon’s orbital region. These objects can remain near Earth for **decades or even centuries** before drifting away due to **gravitational perturbations.**
● Quasi-moons are scientifically important because their proximity makes them ideal **targets for** **asteroid studies**, orbital dynamics research, and potential **future space missions.** Examples include **Kamo****ʻ****oalewa and 2023 FW13.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Astronomers discovered the Arjuna 2025 PN7, a new quasi-satellite of Earth; it was first spotted by the Pan-STARRS 1 telescope in Hawaii.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Global Groupings and Agreements (FTA, Paris Agreement, NPT, QUAD, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Global Plastics Treaty:
I. - It is negotiated under the United Nations
Framework Convention on Climate Change
(UNFCCC).
II. 	 It addresses only the disposal and waste
management stage of plastic pollution.
III. 	 India has extended support for the global
phase-out list and trade restrictions on plastic
products.
How many of the statements given above are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Global Plastics Treaty** is a proposed **international, legally binding agreement** to end plastic pollution globally. Its **mandate was adopted** in **2022** at the **United Nations Environment** **Assembly (UNEA-5.2)**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The treaty mandate explicitly calls for addressing **“the full life cycle of plastics”,** covering extraction, production, use, and disposal. **So, statement II is not correct**.
● India’s negotiating position has aligned with several developing countries and petrochemical-producing states in **opposing global phase-out lists and trade restrictions on plastic products**. India has consistently argued against binding production caps and controls on certain chemicals, citing the right to sustainable development and preference for consensus-based decisions. **So,** **statement III is not** **correct**. **So, none of the statements given above is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Ramsar Sites, World Heritage Sites, UNESCO Heritage Sites, WHS, Biodiversity Heritage Sites, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Exemplary ecological and biological
processes
II. 	 A masterpiece of human creative genius
III. 	 Association with events or ideas of universal
significance
IV. 	 Representation of Earth's evolutionary
history
V. 	 Critical habitats of biological diversity
How many of the above are used as selection
criteria for UNESCO Natural Heritage sites?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **UNESCO World Heritage Sites** are places of **outstanding universal value** recognised for their cultural, natural, or mixed **importance**. They are protected under the **1972 World Heritage** **Convention.** Sites are classified into **Cultural, Natural, and Mixed categories** and are selected based on specific criteria assessed globally.
● **Selection Criteria for Natural Heritage Sites (4 Criteria):** ○ Contains **superlative natural phenomena** or exceptional natural beauty ○ Represents significant ongoing **ecological and biological processes. So, point I is correct.** ○ Represents major stages of **Earth's evolutionary history. So, point IV is correct.** ○ Contains **critical habitats** **for biodiversity** conservation (including threatened species). **So,** **point V is correct.**
● **Selection Criteria for Cultural Heritage Sites (6 Criteria):** ○ Masterpiece of **human creative genius. So, point II is not correct.** ○ Exhibits important **interchange of human values** (architecture, technology, arts, town planning, landscape design) ○ Unique or exceptional testimony to a **cultural tradition** or civilisation ○ Outstanding example of **architecture/technology/landscape** illustrating a stage in human history ○ Outstanding example of **traditional human settlement** or land/sea use ○ Directly associated with **events, ideas, beliefs, or artistic works** of outstanding **universal** significance.  **So, point III is not correct.** **So, only three of the above are used as selection criteria for UNESCO Natural Heritage sites.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Six sites have been added to India's Tentative List by UNESCO's World Heritage Centre in 2025.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Tiger Reserve, Elephant Reserve, other species-specific reserves, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006:
I. - The right of ownership recognised under the
FRA includes timber as part of minor forest
produce.
II. 	 The Divisional Forest Officer (DFO) is the
statutory authority to initiate the process for
determining the nature of forest rights.
III. 	 Free, prior and informed consent of the
Gram Sabha is mandatory for relocation and
resettlement of forest dwellers from forest
land.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **a**
**Explanation:**
● The **Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006,** was enacted to correct historical injustice faced by forestdwelling Scheduled Tribes (STs) and other traditional forest dwellers by recognizing and vesting forest rights and empowering the Gram Sabha as the central authority in the process.
● As per **the FRA**, to qualify as a Forest Dwelling Scheduled Tribe (FDST), the claimant **must be a** **Scheduled Tribe in the area where the right is claimed**, along with having primarily resided in forest land prior to 13-12-2005 and being dependent on forests for bona fide livelihood needs.
● The FRA clearly defines minor forest produce (MFP) as **all non-timber forest produce of plant origin**, including bamboo, tendu/kendu leaves, etc. **Timber is explicitly excluded** from the definition of MFP. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Gram Sabha** is the empowered authority under the FRA to **initiate the process of** **determining the nature and extent of forest rights**. The Divisional Forest Officer (DFO) does not have statutory authority to initiate this process. **So statement II is not correct.**
● The **FRA requires obtaining free, prior, and informed consent (FPIC**) from Gram Sabhas before any resettlement or relocation is undertaken. The provisions mandate the settlement of rights in targeted resettlement areas and ensure the active participation of affected communities before any relocation occurs. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Mass Extinctions and IUCN Red List
questions.questionText:
Consider the following species:
I. - Pygmy Hog
II. 	 Indus River Dolphin
III. 	 Amur Falcon
IV. 	 Asian Giant Tortoise
V. 	 Lion-tailed Macaque
How many of the above are classified as Critically
Endangered in the IUCN Red List?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Pygmy Hog** is the **world’s smallest wild pig**, endemic to **Assam’s tall grasslands**, mainly Manas National Park and nearby areas. It is classified as **Critically Endangered (IUCN)** due to habitat loss and burning of grasslands. It is protected under **Schedule I of the Wildlife Protection Act** **(WPA), 1972,** and conservation is supported by the Pygmy Hog Conservation Programme (PHCP). **So, point I is correct.**
● The **Indus River Dolphin** is found mainly in the **Indus River system in Pakistan** and is a subspecies of the South Asian river dolphin. It is classified as **Endangered (IUCN), not Critically Endangered.** Major threats include **water diversion**, **dam construction**, pollution, and accidental entanglement in fishing nets. It is one of the few **freshwater dolphins** and uses **echolocation** due to poor eyesight. **So, point II is not correct.**
● The **Amur Falcon** is a small **migratory raptor** famous for its long-distance migration from Siberia/China to southern Africa. It is classified as **Least Concern (IUCN).** In India, it is strongly associated with **Nagaland (Doyang Lake),** where large roosting populations occur. It is known for feeding heavily on insects like **locusts and dragonflies** during migration. **So, point III is not correct.**
● The **Asian Giant Tortoise** is classified as **Critically Endangered (IUCN)** due to illegal wildlife trade, habitat **destruction**, and **hunting**. It is found in parts of Northeast India, Southeast Asia, and forested habitats. It is among **Asia’s largest tortoise species.** It is **protected under wildlife laws** and is also impacted by shifting cultivation and forest fragmentation. **So, point IV is correct.**
● The **Lion-tailed Macaque** is endemic to the **Western Ghats**, especially the evergreen rainforests of Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu. It is classified as **Endangered (IUCN), not Critically** **Endangered.** Major threats include habitat fragmentation, plantation expansion, and road development. It is one of **India’s most iconic primates** and is protected under Schedule I of the WPA, 1972.  **So,** **point V is not correct.** **So, only two of the above species are classified as Critically Endangered in the IUCN Red List.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India’s pygmy hog is disappearing from its native grasslands at a time when its conservation is more critical than ever.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Aquatic Ecosystem and Life (Aquatic Ecosystem, Maritime Zones, Corals, Mangrove, Marine Vegetation)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Carbon Description
I. - Red Biological material accumulated on snow and ice surfaces
II. - Brown Heat-trapping organic aerosols mainly from biomass burning
III. - Purple Carbon stored in coastal and marine ecosystems
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● Carbon exists in multiple forms, often described using **colour-based classifications**, each reflecting its source, function and environmental impact.
● **Red carbon** refers to **biological material,** such as algae and microbes, that accumulate **on snow and** **ice surfaces.** These organisms darken the surface, reduce albedo, and increase absorption of solar radiation, thereby accelerating the melting of glaciers and ice sheets. **So, pair I is correctly matched**.
● **Brown carbon consists** of light-absorbing **organic aerosols** primarily released from biomass burning, including forest fires, agricultural residue burning, and residential wood use. These particles **trap heat** **and contribute to atmospheric warming**, especially in regions prone to wildfires. **So, pair II is** **correctly matched**.
● **Purple carbon** represents carbon captured from **industrial emissions** and reused in products, highlighting the role of technological innovation and circular economy approaches in reducing greenhouse gas impacts.
○ The carbon stored in coastal and marine ecosystems like mangroves, seagrass meadows, and salt marshes is referred to as **blue carbon**, not purple carbon. **So, pair III is not correctly** **matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Black carbon**, released from incomplete combustion of fossil fuels and biomass, strongly absorbs sunlight, accelerates atmospheric warming and hastens ice and snow melt.
● **Green carbon** stored in forests and vegetation acts as a major carbon sink by absorbing carbon dioxide through photosynthesis, while also supporting biodiversity and regulating the water cycle.
● **Blue carbon**, found in coastal and marine ecosystems such as mangroves, seagrasses and salt marshes, is highly efficient in long-term carbon storage and protects coastlines from erosion.
● **Teal carbon**, a more recent concept, refers to carbon stored in non-tidal freshwater wetlands like marshes, peatlands and lakes, which play a key role in climate regulation, water purification and biodiversity conservation.
○ India’s first study on **teal carbon**, conducted at **Keoladeo National Park,** **Bharatpur**, highlights the critical role of **inland freshwater wetlands** in climate adaptation and resilience.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are possible environmental
impacts of rare earth mining?
I. - Radioactive contamination
II. 	 Acid mine drainage
III. 	 Release of sulphur dioxide and nitrogen
oxide
IV. 	 Coral reef degradation
V. 	 Arsenic contamination of groundwater
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:

options.d: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Rare Earth Element (REE) mining** is environmentally sensitive because it involves processing ores containing **radioactive minerals** and **toxic** chemicals. Extraction and refining often generate hazardous waste, **pollute air and water,** and cause long-term **ecological damage**, especially when **safety** **regulations** are weak.
● Rare earth ores often occur along with **radioactive elements** like **thorium** and **uranium**. During mining and processing, **radioactive tailings** may accumulate in waste dumps. If not safely contained, **radiation** can **contaminate soil and water,** creating long-term health and environmental hazards. **So, point I is correct.**
● Mining exposes **sulfide minerals** to air and water, producing **sulfuric acid.** This **acidic runoff** dissolves heavy metals and carries them into nearby **rivers** and **groundwater**. **Acid mine drainage** reduces water quality, harms **aquatic ecosystems**, and makes land unfit for agriculture. **So, point II** **is correct.**
● Processing of rare earth ores involves **roasting** and **chemical** treatment, which can **emit sulphur** **dioxide (SO**₂**) and oxides of nitrogen (NOx).** These gases contribute to **air pollution, acid rain**, and respiratory diseases. They also **damage crops, forests, and water** bodies through acidification. **So, point III is correct.**
● Rare earth extraction is also linked with **deep-sea mining of polymetallic nodules**, which contain REEs and other strategic metals. Such **seabed mining** can create sediment plumes, **disturb marine** **habitats,** and reduce water quality. These disturbances can **stress coral ecosystems** and contribute indirectly to **coral bleaching** and **reef degradation**. **So, point IV is correct.**
● Rare earth mining can release **toxic heavy metals like arsenic**, especially when **chemical leaching** is used. Arsenic can seep into **groundwater sources**, causing **severe health issues** like skin diseases, cancers, and organ damage, and making **drinking water unsafe** for communities. **So, point V is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Union Budget 2026–27 announced the establishment of dedicated Rare Earth Corridors in four coastal states to strengthen India’s critical minerals ecosystem.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Kaziranga National Park:
I. - It is one of the largest undisturbed floodplain grasslands in South Asia.
II. 	 It hosts one of the highest tiger densities in India.
III. 	 It is recognised as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
IV. 	 Brahmaputra River flows along the northern boundary of the park.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Kaziranga National Park is located in **Assam**. The **Brahmaputra River** flows along the **northern** **boundary of the park,** and the Karbi Anglong Hills (Mikir Hills) form its southern boundary. **So,** **statement IV is correct**.
● It represents one of the **largest undisturbed and representative floodplain grasslands of the** **Brahmaputra Valley**, making it one of the most significant floodplain grassland ecosystems in South Asia**. So, statement I is correct**.
● The park has recorded **one of the highest tiger densities in India** and has been declared a **Tiger** **Reserve**, owing to its high prey biomass and suitable habitat. **So, statement II is correct**.
● It was inscribed as a United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization **(UNESCO)** **World Heritage Site in 1985** for its **natural values**, particularly biodiversity and ecological processes. **So, statement III is correct**.
● The park is the **world s largest stronghold of the one-horned rhinoceros (Rhinoceros** **unicornis)**.
● It hosts **significant populations of tigers, elephants, wild water buffalo, swamp deer, and** **migratory birds**. **So, all four of the statements given above are correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Director of Kaziranga National Park has become the first Indian to receive the International Union for Conservation of Nature award for innovation in national park management.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText: Despite high deposits, a bank may not lend money to businesses and individuals. This situation most likely indicates:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● **Credit-deposit ratio** is the ratio of how much a bank lends out of the deposits it has mobilized. It indicates how much of a bank's core funds are being used for lending, the main banking activity.
● **A higher ratio indicates more reliance on deposits for lending and vice-versa. A very low** **ratio indicates banks are not making full use of their resources.** If the ratio is above a certain level, it indicates pressure on resources. Thus, if there are high deposits in a bank and it still does not lend money to businesses or individuals, it indicates a low credit-deposit ratio.
● **Capital output ratio is the amount of capital needed to produce one unit of output.** It explains the relationship between the level of investment and the corresponding economic growth. A lower capital-output ratio shows that only a low level of investment is needed to produce a given growth rate in the economy.
● **Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR)** is the percentage of a bank's deposit that it needs to maintain as reserves with the central bank. The cash reserve is either stored in the bank’s vault or sent to the Reserve Bank of India (RBI).
● When the CRR rate is low, the banks have more money to provide loans to a large number of businesses and industries for investment purposes.
● Thus, the lower the CRR, the higher liquidity with the banks, which in turn goes into investment and lending and vice-versa. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the insurance sector in India, consider the following statements:
I. - India is among the top ten insurance markets in the world in terms of nominal premium volumes.
II. 	 India permits 100% aggregate foreign ownership of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), Foreign Portfolio Investment (FPI), and other foreign investors.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● As per the Swiss Re report, **India is the 10th largest insurance market** globally by **nominal** **premium volumes** in 2024, with a market share of 1.8 per cent. Insurance **penetration** stood at 3.7 per cent, with **life insurance** at 2.7 per cent and non-life at 1 per cent, while insurance density **increased marginally** to USD 97, indicating significant untapped market potential. **So, statement I** **is correct.**
● The **Insurance Amendment Act, 2025,** permits the **100% investment limit** to the **aggregate** **foreign ownership of FDI, FPI, and other foreign investors** taken together, and not to separate limits for each category.  **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Under the **Sabka Bima Sabki Raksha (Amendment of Insurance Laws) Act, 2025,** or the Insurance Amendment Act of 2025, the governance requirements were liberalised. **Only one** **of the top leadership positions** (Chairperson, CEO or Managing Director) in insurance companies must be held by resident Indian citizens. **Earlier rules required that the majority** of its directors, KMPs and at least one among the Chief Executive Officer (CEO)/ Managing Director (MD)/ Chairperson of its Board must be a **resident Indian citizen.**
**Relevance:** The Parliament of India has recently passed the Sabka Bima, Sabki Raksha (Amendment of Insurance Laws) Bill, 2025, proposing to raise the Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) limit in the insurance sector from 74% to 100%.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and Neighbourhood (Pakistan, China, Nepal, Sri Lanka, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With regard to fertilizers in India, consider the following statements:
I. - India is the third-largest producer and secondlargest consumer of fertilizers globally.
II. 	 About 90 percent of NPK complex fertilizers used in India are produced domestically, though most raw materials are imported.
III. 	 In the nano-fertilizer segment, only nano urea is currently available and used in the Indian market.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **India** is among the **largest fertilizer-consuming nations** due to its extensive agricultural base and high **cropping intensity.** Globally, India is recognized as the **second-largest consumer** of fertilizers after China. It is also the **third-largest producer.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● India has achieved nearly **90% self-sufficiency in the production of NPK complex fertilizers.** However, the country remains highly dependent on imports for the underlying raw materials, such as rock phosphate and phosphoric acid, and is 100% dependent on imports for potash. **So, statement II** **is correct.**
● Nano fertilizers in India are **not limited to nano urea** alone. Along with nano urea, **nano DAP** has also been **introduced and promoted** through campaigns such as the **“Maha abhiyan”** across agroclimatic zones. Nano urea and nano DaP are now available at **Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samridhi** **Kendras (PMKSKs)** across the country. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India recorded its highest-ever domestic urea production in 2023–24, crossing 314 lakh metric tonnes.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText: Which one of the following has accounted for the highest exports by India in terms of value during the last five years?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During the **last five years**, **rice** has consistently been **India’s largest agricultural export** in value terms, outperforming all other agri-commodities. **Both basmati and non-basmati** rice have contributed the **highest share** to India’s agricultural exports, often **exceeding US$ 10–12 billion** annually in peak years such as FY23 and FY25.
● **Rice** alone accounts for **more than 30%** of India’s total agricultural exports, reflecting India’s position as the world’s **leading rice exporter.** While **marine** products, especially frozen shrimp, rank second with exports around **US$ 7–8 billion,** and **sugar** has shown a strong but **volatile** performance due to **export controls**, neither matches rice in sustained export value. **Spices**, though globally significant, contribute a **comparatively smaller share**.
● **Major destinations for Indian rice** include **West Asia, Africa, Bangladesh, China, and the** **United States.** Despite periodic export restrictions on non-basmati rice, rice continues to dominate **India’s agri-export basket** due to high production, global demand, and competitive pricing. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India exported rice worth $391.74 million to the US in FY25.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Balance of Payments (BoP)
questions.questionText:
With regard to Non-Performing Assets (NPAs) in India, consider the following statements:
I. - A loan is generally classified as a nonperforming asset if the principal or interest remains overdue for more than 60 days.
II. 	 In the case of agricultural loans for longduration crops, an asset is classified as NPA if the interest or installment remains overdue for one crop season.
III. 	 The National Asset Reconstruction Company Limited (NARCL) proposes to acquire stressed assets by paying 15% in cash and 85% in the form of Security Receipts.
IV. 	 The India Debt Resolution Company Limited (IDRCL) is responsible for managing and undertaking value addition of stressed assets acquired by NARCL.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **Non-Performing Asset (NPA)** refers to a bank loan or advance that stops generating income because **interest or principal repayment** remains **overdue** beyond the RBI-prescribed timeline. NPAs are classified to ensure **realistic income recognition,** accurate **balance sheet** reporting, and **provisioning**.
● As per **Reserve Bank of India’s Income Recognition, Asset Classification, and Provisioning** **(IRaC) norms,** a loan becomes an NPA when **interest and/or installment** of principal remains overdue for **more than 90 days** in case of term loans. Similarly, **OD/CC (Overdraft/Cash Credit)** accounts are treated as NPa if they remain “out of order” **beyond 90 days. So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● RBI provides a special classification method for **direct agricultural advances.** A loan for **long-** **duration** crops becomes an NPA if principal or interest remains **overdue for one crop season**, while loans for **short-duration** crops become NPA if overdue for **two crop seasons,** reflecting agricultural income cycles. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **NARCL** follows a structured resolution model where **stressed assets** are acquired by paying **15%** **upfront in cash,** while the remaining **85%** is paid through **Security Receipts (SRs). So, statement** **III is correct.**
○ These SRs represent a claim on **future recoveries** and are backed by a government guarantee for a limited period.
● **India Debt Resolution Company Limited** (**IDRCL)** acts as the operational **resolution entity for** **NARCL.** It is engaged in **management**, turnaround strategy, **recovery efforts**, and value enhancement of stressed assets acquired by NaRCL.  **So, statement IV is correct.**
○ **This separation** ensures NARCL focuses on acquisition while IDRCL handles **professional** **resolution** and recovery execution. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Indian banks' Gross NPa ratio dropped to a multi-decade low of 2.1% by late 2025, with Net NPA at 0.5%.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText: The London Consensus, sometimes seen in the news, is:

options.a: A set of trade liberalisation and privatisation policies promoted by the World Bank
options.b: A new economic framework emphasising wellbeing, resilience, and state capacity over one-size-fits-all reforms
options.c: A monetary policy agreement among G7 countries to control inflation
options.d: A doctrine advocating minimal state intervention in economic activities

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **London Consensus** is a proposed **new economic framework for the 21st century**, developed by over **50 leading international economists** in 2023.
● It was formulated at the **London School of Economics (LSE)** as an alternative to the **Washington** **Consensus**, whose market-centric prescriptions dominated global economic policy since the 1990s.
● Unlike the Washington Consensus, it moves away from rigid, one-size-fits-all prescriptions and rigid economic dogmas. It emphasises **five core principles**:
○ Focus on **wellbeing beyond GDP**, including social cohesion and trust. ○ Ensure that **economic growth is inclusive and employment-generating**. ○ **Build resilience** through a strong welfare state to manage shocks like pandemics and trade disruptions. ○ **Put politics at the centre of economic policymaking**, viewing it as an enabler rather than a constraint. ○ **Invest in state capacity**, including legal, regulatory, and institutional frameworks.
● The consensus responds to **post-2008 global realities**, including backlash against hyper-globalisation, climate change, technological disruption (AI), and the rise of populism.
● It provides **guiding principles rather than detailed policy prescriptions**, allowing countries to design policies suited to their **own historical and institutional contexts**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - It is a cool temperate climatic zone situated between 45° and 60° latitudes.
II. 	 It is distinguished by the continuous dominance of the Westerlies and mild temperatures.
III. 	 It has substantial precipitation throughout the year with a peak in winter.
Which one of the following best corresponds to the description above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **British Type (Cool Temperate) Climate** occurs between **45° and 60° latitudes in both** **hemispheres**.
● It is found along the **western coasts of continents**, notably in **Western Europe and the British** **Isles**, the **western coast of North America** (Oregon, Washington, British Columbia), **southern** **Chile**, **New Zealand**, and **southeastern Australia (Victoria and Tasmania)**.
● This is a **humid, maritime climate** dominated throughout the year by the **Westerlies**. Rainfall is **well distributed across all months**, but there is a **distinct winter maximum**, while summers receive comparatively less rainfall.
● **Orographic rainfall** is significant on windward mountain slopes, leading to very high annual precipitation in such areas.
● Mean annual temperatures range between **4.4°C and 15.5°C**, with cool summers and mild winters, making the climate **comfortable and conducive to mental alertness**.
● The natural vegetation consists mainly of **temperate deciduous forests**. Important hardwood species include **oak, elm, ash, birch, beech, poplar, and hornbeam**, while **willow, alder, and aspen** grow in wetter areas. Other species such as **chestnut, sycamore, maple, and lime** are also common.
● Economically, this climatic region represents some of the **most urbanised, industrialised, and** **developed parts of the world**. It is well known for **marine fishing** (especially in Britain, Norway, and British Columbia), **intensive market gardening (truck farming)**, **horticulture**, **mixed** **farming**, **dairy farming**, and **sheep rearing**.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following materials:
I. - Coking coal
II. 	 Iron ore
III. 	 Limestone
How many of the above are required to be imported for steel production in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The availability of raw materials is a critical determinant of a country’s steel production capacity and industrial competitiveness. With the Government recently setting a target of 500 million tonnes of steel production by 2034, ensuring secure and affordable access to key inputs has become even more important.
● **Coking coal** is the **only major steel raw material that India imports in large quantities**. Although India has vast coal reserves, most of it is non-coking coal used for power generation. Domestic coking coal has high ash content (often above 25%), whereas efficient blast furnace operations require low-ash coal (around 12%). As a result, India imports nearly **85–90%** of its coking coal requirements, mainly from Australia, the USA, and Russia. **So, point I is correct.**
● India is well endowed with **high-grade iron ore reserves,** particularly in Odisha and Chhattisgarh. It is **generally a net exporter** and among the world’s leading producers of crude steel. **So, point II is** **not correct.**
● **Limestone** acts as a flux in steelmaking to remove impurities such as silica. Despite having large domestic limestone reserves, India **imports high-grade limestone for its steel industry** because more than 95% of domestic production is of cement-grade quality, which is unsuitable for specialised metallurgical use. **So, point III is correct.** **So, only two of the above are required to be imported for India’s steel production industry.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Coffee cultivation in India is largely concentrated in Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu.
**Statement II:** The Western Ghats provide high rainfall, a humid tropical climate, forested shade cover, and suitable elevation.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation for Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II is **not** the correct explanation for Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Coffee cultivation in India is **concentrated in Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu** because these regions offer **distinct agro-ecological advantages**. **Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu** together account for about **96% of production**.  **So, statement I is correct.**
● These states are located in the **Western Ghats,** which provide ideal conditions for **Arabica**, which thrives at **higher elevations (1000–1500 m)** with **cooler temperatures (15–25°C)**, moderate humidity, and higher rainfall, producing **mild, aromatic coffee**. **Robusta** is better suited to **lower** **elevations (500–1000 m)** with **warmer, humid conditions (20–30°C)**, slightly lower rainfall, and yields **strong, full-bodied coffee**.  **So, statement II is correct.** **So,  both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation** **for Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Coffee in India began around **1600 AD** when **Baba Budan** brought **seven coffee** **beans from Yemen** and planted them in the **Bababudangiris (Karnataka)**.
● The country holds **GI tags** for coffees such as **Coorg Arabica (Karnataka),** **Wayanad Robusta (Kerala), Chikmagalur Arabica (Karanataka), Araku** **Valley Arabica (Andhra Pradesh and Odisha), Bababudangiris Arabica** **(Karanataka)**, and the specialty **Monsooned Malabar (Karnataka and Kerala)**.
● **The Coffee Board of India** was established under the **Coffee Act, 1942**, which drives research, quality improvement, export promotion, and infrastructure through schemes like the **Integrated Coffee Development Project**.

**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:**
● For the first time in nearly 50 years, India’s life expectancy at birth has declined, falling by 0.2 years to
69.8 years during 2017–21 compared to 2016–20. The decline was 0.1 years in rural areas and 0.3 years in urban areas. According to  **Sample Registration System (**SRS)-based Abridged Life Tables (2017– 21), females continue to outlive males by over two years at birth, and by about one year even at age 70.
● **Kerala** consistently records the **highest life expectancy** at birth in India, with the latest SRS data and government reports indicating an average life expectancy of about **74.9 years**.
● This high longevity is primarily attributed to the state's robust public healthcare system, high literacy rates (especially among women), and strong emphasis on social welfare and sanitation. While other states have improved, Kerala has maintained its lead in the "at birth" category for decades **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText: The Hayli Gubbi volcano, which recently erupted, is a:

options.a: Stratovolcano located in Indonesia at a convergent plate boundary
options.b: Shield volcano located in the Danakil Depression of Ethiopia
options.c: Caldera volcano located in Chile along the Andes
options.d: Composite volcano located in Japan along the Pacific Ring of Fire

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Hayli Gubbi shield volcano** in the **Danakil** **(Afar)** **Depression** **of** **northern** **Ethiopia** experienced its **first documented explosive** **eruption recently**.
● Tectonically, the event is linked to the **divergent** **plate boundary** in the Afar region, where crustal stretching allows magma to rise. Hayli Gubbi lies close to **Erta Ale**, Ethiopia’s most active volcano. Preeruption signals included **sulphur** **dioxide** **emissions, summit clouds, and ground uplift**.
● Geological evidence highlights the **dynamic and** **interconnected nature of volcanism** in the Afar Rift system. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The **Afar region** lies in **northeastern Ethiopia** and includes the **Afar (Danakil)** **Depression**, a vast arid zone characterised by **desert scrubland, shallow saline** **lakes, and long chains of active volcanoes**.
● The **Awash River valley** forms the southern part of the region; notably, the river **does not reach the sea** but drains into inland lakes within the depression.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Weak western disturbances
II. 	 Southward shift of the western disturbance
III. 	 Higher diurnal temperature variation
How many of the reasons given above have
contributed to the recent snowfall deficit in the
Western Himalayan region?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The winter of 2025–26 has been unusually dry across India, with the **north-west region receiving** **only about 8% of its normal rainfall**, making the western Himalayan states largely snowless.
● **Uttarakhand recorded zero rainfall in December–January**, Himachal Pradesh saw its **sixth-** **lowest December rainfall since 1901**, and Jammu & Kashmir experienced **very little rain and** **snowfall in January**, as per India Meteorological Department (IMD) data.
● Winter precipitation in north and northwest India is mainly caused by **western disturbances** interacting with moisture-laden winds from the Arabian Sea or Bay of Bengal. This year, although **more** **WDs than normal passed over the region**, they were **moisture-deficient, shallow, and weak**, limiting their ability to lift moisture and produce precipitation. **So, point I is correct.**
● The **northward** shift in the tracks of western disturbance **(not southward)** at higher latitudes resulted in precipitation over Kashmir and parts of Himachal, while **Uttarakhand remained dry**. **So, point II** **is not correct.**
● Even when snowfall is delayed to February, **higher daytime temperatures and large diurnal** **temperature variation** reduce snow persistence. Early winter snowfall normally allows gradual melting and better moisture retention, but delayed snow melts faster, reducing benefits to agriculture and groundwater recharge. **So, point III is correct.** **So, only two of the reasons given above have contributed to the recent snowfall deficit in the** **Western Himalayan region.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
urea:
I. - It is essential for chlorophyll formation and
vegetative development.
II. 	 Its excessive use can contribute to
eutrophication of water bodies.
III. 	 Natural gas is the primary feedstock
for synthesising ammonia used in urea
manufacture.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Urea (carbamide) is the most widely used **nitrogenous fertiliser** in India, containing **46% nitrogen**, essential for plant growth. It is also a naturally occurring metabolic waste product in humans and animals and a key industrial chemical.
● Industrially, urea is produced using **ammonia synthesised primarily from natural gas**, making natural gas the **key feedstock** in urea manufacture. **So, statement III is correct**.
● In agriculture, urea is the **most widely used nitrogenous fertilizer**. Nitrogen supplied by urea is **essential for chlorophyll formation**, photosynthesis, and **vegetative growth** of plants. **So,** **statement I is correct**.
● Excessive use of urea can lead to **nitrogen runoff** into nearby water bodies. This nutrient enrichment causes **eutrophication**, leading to rapid algal growth (algal blooms) that depletes dissolved oxygen and harms aquatic life. **So statement II is correct**.
● In India, policy measures such as **neem-coated urea** and **Nano Urea** have been introduced to improve nitrogen-use efficiency and reduce environmental damage, making urea an important topic at the intersection of agriculture, energy, and environmental sustainability. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the election of the Vice President of India:
I. - The election is conducted through an open ballot system.
II. 	 The value of votes of Members of Parliament in the Vice-Presidential election varies based on the population of the state they represent.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **office of the Vice President of India** is established under Article 63 of the Constitution of India.
● The **Vice President of India** is elected through an **indirect election** by an **electoral college** **consisting of members of both Houses of Parliament**.
● By established convention and the Presidential and Vice-Presidential Elections Rules, 1974, the **Secretary-General of the Lok Sabha or the Rajya Sabha acts as the Returning Officer by** **rotation** for conducting the Vice-Presidential election. This is a well-established parliamentary convention.
● Under Article 66(1) of the Constitution of India, the **Vice-Presidential election is conducted by** **secret ballot**, following the system of **proportional representation by means of the single** **transferable vote (STV)**. An **open ballot system is not used**. **So, statement I is not correct**.
● In the **Vice-Presidential election**, **all Members of Parliament have equal voting value**. There is **no differentiation in vote value based on state population**, unlike the Presidential election, where vote value varies. **So, statement II is not correct**.
● All **doubts and disputes arising out of or in connection with the election of the Vice** **President are decided exclusively by the Supreme Court of India**, and its decision is final. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts on the Election of the Vice President of India:**
● The Vice President is elected by an **electoral college consisting of both elected** **and nominated members of Parliament**.
● **State Legislative Assemblies do not participate** in the Vice-Presidential election.
● The election follows: ○ **Proportional representation** ○ **Single Transferable Vote (STV)** ○ **Secret ballot**
● The election **cannot be challenged** merely because of a vacancy in the electoral college.
● If the Supreme Court later declares the election void, **acts performed by the Vice** **President before such declaration remain valid**.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Article 249 of the Constitution of India reflects the quasi-federal nature of the Indian Federation.
**Statement II:** Article 249 allows Parliament to legislate on State List subjects in the national interest.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 249** of the Constitution of India clearly represents a **temporary departure from the federal** **distribution of legislative powers**, allowing Parliament to legislate on matters in the **State List** only when the **Rajya Sabha declares, by a special majority, that it is necessary or expedient in the** **national interest**. **So, statements I and II are correct.**
● Under **Article 249(3)**, a law enacted by Parliament on the basis of a **Rajya Sabha resolution** does **not cease immediately** upon the expiry of the one-year period of the resolution. Such a law continues to remain in force for a **further period of six months after the resolution has ceased to be in** **force**, except with respect to acts already done or omitted to be done. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText: With reference to Local Self-Government in India, which one of the following statements is **not** correct?

options.a: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, mandates a three-tier system of local governance in all States.
options.b: In Sixth Schedule areas, Autonomous District Councils have powers to levy and collect specified local taxes and fees.
options.c: The State Government has substantial administrative, legislative and financial control over local bodies.
options.d: In Fifth Schedule areas, the Governor may modify the application of State laws relating to local governance.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Local Self-Government in India is a system of decentralised governance institutionalised by the Seventythird and Seventy-fourth Constitutional Amendment Acts, 1992, to promote grassroots democracy and local development.
● The **73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendment Acts, 1992**, provide for a **three-tier system of** **local governance**. However, States with a population **not exceeding 20 lakh** are not required to constitute the intermediate tier. Further, these provisions do not automatically apply to certain States such as Nagaland, Meghalaya, and Mizoram, and to specific tribal and hill areas.
● Under the **Sixth Schedule**, Autonomous District Councils in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram are empowered to administer local affairs and levy and collect specified taxes and fees.
● **State Governments** exercise substantial administrative, legislative, and financial control over local bodies, which are a **State subject** under the Seventh Schedule. They **oversee grievance redressal** **mechanisms,** regulate personnel matters, and possess supervisory powers such as inspection, cancellation of resolutions, and supersession of local bodies.
● In **Fifth Schedule** areas, the Governor is empowered to direct, by public notification, that any law of Parliament or the State Legislature shall not apply, or shall apply with modifications, to Scheduled Areas. **Therefore, option (a) is the answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following languages:
I. - Kashmiri
II. 	 Konkani
III. 	 Marathi
IV. 	 Nepali
How many of the above were added to the Eighth
Schedule through Constitutional Amendments?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Eighth Schedule of the Constitution of India** originally contained **14 languages** at the time of the commencement of the Constitution of India in 1950.
● **The Eighth Schedule of the Constitution** lists the languages recognised by the Constitution of India and currently contains **22 languages**. Initially, only **14 languages** were included in 1950.
● **Kashmiri** was one of the **original 14 languages i**ncluded in the **Eighth Schedule of the** **Constitution of India, when it was adopted in 1950. So, point I is not correct.**
● **Konkani** was added to the Eighth Schedule by the **71st Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992**. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Marathi** was actually included in the original 14 languages of the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution of India in 1950, **not added later. So, point III is not correct.**
● **Nepali** was added to the Eighth Schedule by the **71st Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992**, along with Konkani and Manipuri.  **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Sindhi** was added by the **21st Constitutional Amendment Act, 1967**.
● **Manipuri** was added by the **71st Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992**.
● **Bodo, Dogri, Maithili, and Santhali** were added by the **92nd Constitutional Amendment Act,** **2003**. **So, only two of the above were added to the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution of India by** **Constitutional Amendments.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

With reference to the Tata Steel Chess Tournament,

of Chess”.
II. 	 D. Gukesh won the Masters title at its 88th
edition held in 2026.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Tata Steel Chess Tournament** is one of the **oldest and most prestigious chess** **tournaments in the world**, with its origins dating back to **1938**.  It was originally known as the **Hoogovens Tournament**.
● It is popularly known as the **“Wimbledon of Chess”**, reflecting its elite status, long tradition, and high level of competition. **So, statement I is correct**.
● The tournament is held **annually in Wijk aan Zee, the Netherlands.**
● **Nodirbek Abdusattorov** (Uzbekistan) secured the Master's title after defeating Arjun Erigaisi in the final round in the 88th edition of the Tata Steel Chess Tournament. **So, statement II is not correct**.
● Magnus Carlsen has won the Tata Steel Chess Tournament a record number of times, making him the most successful player in its history. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
