questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Advent of Europeans, British Expansion, Anglo Wars
questions.questionText:
Consider the following leaders:
I. - Chittaranjan Das
II. 	 Lala Lajpat Rai
III. 	 Gopal Krishna Gokhale
IV. 	 Aurobindo Ghosh
V. 	 Madan Mohan Malaviya
Which of the above were associated with the
Home Rule League Movement?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Home Rule League Movement (1916–1918)** was launched by **Bal Gangadhar Tilak** and **Annie Besant** to demand **self-government f**or India within the British Empire. It expanded political awareness, **mobilised educated Indians,** and influenced reforms like the **Montagu Declaration** **(1917).**
● **Chittaranjan Das supported** the Home Rule agitation and was among the prominent nationalist leaders who **joined the movement** during its expansion phase. His participation strengthened the **movement in Bengal** and helped spread its **political message** among educated Indians. **So, point I** **is correct.**
● **Lala Lajpat Rai** was **associated** with the Home Rule League Movement and supported its demand for **Swarajya**. Though primarily linked with **extremist nationalism,** he contributed to **political** **mobilisation** and helped strengthen the national movement during the First World War period. **So,** **point II is correct.**
● **Gopal Krishna Gokhale** was **not directly associated** with the Home Rule League Movement, as he **passed away in 1915**, before the leagues were established in 1916. However, some members of his **Servants of India Society** later joined the movement. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Aurobindo Ghosh** was not associated with the Home Rule League Movement. By **1910**, he had **withdrawn from active politics** and settled in Pondicherry, focusing on spiritual pursuits. Therefore, he did not participate in the **Home Rule political campaign. So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Madan Mohan Malaviya** was **associated** with the Home Rule League Movement and supported **constitutional nationalist efforts.** Though a moderate leader, he backed initiatives aimed at **self-** **governance** and contributed to **political unity** during the period of Home Rule agitation. **So, point** **V is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Extension of separate communal electorates
II. 	 Grant of provincial autonomy
III. 	 Grant of parliamentary responsibility at the Centre
IV. 	 Separation of Sindh from Bombay
V. 	 Indianisation of the Indian Army
Which of the above were recommendations of the Simon Commission?

options.c: I, II, IV and V only
options.d: I, III, IV and V only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Simon Commission (1927)**, chaired by Sir John Simon, was an **all-white statutory** **commission** appointed to review the **Government of India Act, 1919**. It faced nationwide boycott and later suggested constitutional reforms.
● **Major Recommendations of the Simon Commission:** ○ **Abolition of dyarchy** in provinces. ○ **Provincial autonomy** and expansion of provincial legislatures. **So, point II is correct.** ○ **Governor’s discretionary powers** for internal security and administration. ○ **No parliamentary responsibility at the Centre**; the Governor-General to control cabinet formation. **So, point III is not correct.** ○ **Retention and extension of separate communal electorates** (temporary). **So, point I is** **correct.** ○ **No universal adult franchise.** ○ **Proposal for federalism**, but not immediately. ○ Creation of a **Consultative Council of Greater India** (British provinces + princely states). ○ **Separate Sindh from the Bombay Presidency. So, point IV is correct.** ○ **Separate Burma from India.** ○ Provide **local legislatures for the NWFP and Baluchistan** and representation at the Centre. ○ **Indianisation of the Indian Army**, while retaining British forces for defence. **So, point V is** **correct.** ○ Increased control of the Government of India over **High Courts.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Literature of Ancient India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following events:
I. - Kurnool Circular
II. 	 Operation Zero Hour
III. 	 Establishment of the Prati Sarkar in Satara
IV. 	 Flag Satyagraha
How many of the above were launched as a part of the Quit India Movement (1942)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Quit India Movement**, launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1942 with the call of “Do or Die,” was a nationwide mass uprising against British rule. Triggered by the failure of the Cripps Mission, the hardships of the Second World War, and rising nationalist sentiment, it led to widespread protests, strikes, and the growth of underground resistance across India.
● **Kurnool Circular:** Issued in **1942,** in **Andhra Pradesh**, this **circular** outlined a detailed, **non-** **violent**, and, in later stages, **disruptive program** (cutting telegraph lines, non-payment of taxes) to be followed during the upcoming **Quit India Movement. So, point I is correct.**
● **Operation Zero Hour:** This was the code name for the **swift, surprise action** taken by the **British** **government** in the early hours of August 9, 1942, to **arrest Gandhi** and all **major Congress leaders** immediately after the launch of the **Quit India resolution. So, point II is correct.**
● **Establishment of the Prati Sarkar in Satara**: Following the **crackdown of the Quit India** **Movement**, a parallel, **independent government (Prati Sarkar)** was established in the Satara district of **Maharashtra** in **1942–43**, led by figures like **Nana Patil**, which functioned for over three years. **So, point III is correct.**
● **The Flag Satyagraha in Bilaspur commenced on March 31, 1923,** as a major act of civil disobedience against British rule in Chhattisgarh. Led by local leaders including Pandit Sunderlal and later Mahatma Bhagwandin, volunteers hoisted the national flag in public spaces, defying authorities and fostering regional national sentiment. **So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only three of the above were associated with the Quit India Movement (1942).** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
With reference to Sir Syed Ahmed Khan, consider the following statements:
I. - He established the United Indian Patriotic Association to oppose the Indian National Congress.
II. 	 He was opposed to the introduction of western scientific education among Muslims.
III. 	 He established a Scientific Society for the translation of literature from English to Urdu.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Sir Syed Ahmed was a liberal, modern socio-religious reformer born in 1817 who served the British administration before the revolt of 1857. **United Indian Patriotic association was a political** **organization founded in 1888 by Sir Syed Ahmed Khan and Raja Shiv Prasad Singh of** **Benares.** It **opposed the Indian National Congress** and aimed to develop closer ties between Muslim community and the British Raj. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Sir Syed Ahmed Khan **propagated western scientific education among the Muslim masses**. He wanted to reconcile western scientific education with the teachings in the Quran. **So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● He **established the Scientific Society** in 1864 to spread western education among Muslims through Urdu translation. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
The sculptural panel depicting “Ravana shaking
Mount Kailasa” is one of the most famous artistic
masterpieces found at:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Ravana shaking Mount Kailasa** panel at the **Kailashnath** **Temple** (Cave 16), **Ellora**, is one of the **finest masterpieces of** **Indian rock-cut sculpture**, dated to the **8th century CE**. Though the theme appears in several Ellora caves, this depiction is the **most** **monumental and sophisticated**.
● The sculpture portrays the mythological episode of **Ravana** **attempting to lift Mount Kailasa**, the abode of **Lord Shiva**. The composition is organised into **multiple tiers**, creating a powerful sense of movement and drama.
● In the **lower tier**, Ravana is shown **multi-armed and multi-faced**, effortlessly shaking the mountain. His **angular posture**, thrust leg, and deeply carved arms create a striking **three-dimensional effect**.
● The **upper tier** depicts **Shiva and Parvati**, with Parvati shown moving closer to Shiva in fear, producing a dramatic **play of light and shadow**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Buddhism, Jainism & Other Philosophies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Cave Location Associated
Religion
I. - Nagarjuni Bihar Ajivika
II. - Pitalkhora Maharashtra Jainism
III. - Udayagiri Madhya
Pradesh Hinduism
IV. - Nagarjunakonda Andhra
Pradesh Buddhism
In how many of the above rows is the given
information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Nagarjuni Caves** are a group of three **ancient rock-cut caves** located in the Jehanabad district of **Bihar**, India, dating back to the **3rd century BC.** Situated near the **Barabar Hills,** they were dedicated by Dasharatha Maurya, the grandson of Ashoka, to the **Ajivika sect.** The caves feature remarkable, highly polished granite walls and contain important inscriptions. **So, row I is correctly** **matched.**
● The **Pitalkhora Caves** are an ancient group of **14 rock-cut Buddhist monuments** located in the Satmala range of the **Western Ghats** in **Maharashtra**. Dating back as far as the **3rd to 2nd century** **BCE,** they are among the earliest examples of rock-cut architecture in India, predating the more famous **Ajanta and Ellora** complexes. **So, row II is not correctly matched.**
● The **Udayagiri Caves** are situated near **Vidisha** in **Madhya Pradesh**. Dating to the **Gupta period** (4th–5th century CE), these caves are among the earliest **Hindu rock-cut monuments.** They are famous for **Vaishnavite iconography**, including the monumental Varaha (boar) incarnation of Vishnu. **So, row III is correctly matched.**
● The **Nagarjunakonda** site lies in present-day **Andhra Pradesh** along the Krishna River valley. It was a major center of **Mahayana Buddhism** between the 3rd and 6th centuries CE. The region contains remains of **monasteries**, **stupas**, inscriptions, and Buddhist sculptures. **So, row IV is correctly** **matched.** **So, in only three of the above rows is the given information correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following sites of Major Rock Edicts
of Ashoka:
I. - Kalsi
II. 	 Sopara
III. 	 Sannati
IV. 	 Erragudi
How many of the above are situated south of the
Narmada River?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Major Rock Edicts of Ashoka** are large inscriptions engraved on natural rock surfaces using chisels and hammers. They are not royal proclamations in a narrow sense but represent Ashoka’s **direct** **moral dialogue with his subjects**, projecting him as an upholder of **Dhamma** and “Beloved of the Gods.”
● **Kalsi** is situated in Uttarakhand  **(north of the Narmada River).** Here, 14 rock edicts were found in one place. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **Sopara** was an ancient port city known as **Shurparaka in present-day Maharashtra (south of the** **Narmada River).** The Ashokan edict at Sopara is in **Bramhi script. So, point II is correct.**
● **Sannati** is an ancient Buddhist site located near **Kanaganahalli** in **Kalaburagi district, Karnataka** **(south of the Narmada River)**. **So, point III is correct.**
● **The Yerragudi (or Erragudi)** Rock Edicts are located near the **village of Yerragudi, in the** **Kurnool district of Andhra Pradesh (south of the Narmada River),** India, near the Karnataka border. **So, point IV is correct.**
**So, only three of the above Major Rock Edicts of Ashoka are situated south of the Narmada River.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText: Which one of the following monuments was constructed the earliest?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Brihadisvara Temple**, also known as **Peruvudaiyar Kovil**, stands as a landmark of **Dravidian** **architecture** and an architectural epoch in the history of **Thanjavur**. Built around **1010 CE** by **Rajaraja Chola I**, the temple is dedicated to **Lord Shiva**, worshipped in the form of a massive **Lingam**.
○ The temple is among the **Great** **Living** **Chola** **Temples**, along with **Gangaikondacholeeswaram Temple** and **Airavatesvara Temple**, and is recognised as a **UNESCO World Heritage Site**.
● The **Badami Cave Temples** are a group of **4 rock-cut temples** located at **Badami (ancient** **Vatapi)** in **north-central Karnataka**, near the **Malaprabha River**. Badami was the **capital of the** **Early Chalukyas** in the **6th–7th centuries CE**.
○ The caves were **completed by Mangalesha (598–610 CE)**. **Three caves are Brahmanical** (Shaiva–Vaishnava) and **one is Jain**, reflecting religious tolerance. They are carved into sandstone cliffs opposite **Badami Fort**. ○ **Cave I (Shaiva)**: Nataraja (18-armed dancing Shiva), Ganesha, Ardhanarishvara. ○ **Cave II (Vaishnava)**: Trivikrama and Varaha panels. ○ **Cave III (largest)**: Shaiva–Vaishnava themes; inscription of Mangalesha (578 CE).
○ **Cave IV (Jain)**: Mahavira and Tirthankaras.
● The **Sun Temple, Konark,** is a monumental 13th-century temple located on the **Bay of Bengal coast** **in Odisha**, dedicated to **Surya, the Sun God**. Built during the reign of **Narasimha Deva I** of the Eastern Ganga dynasty (c. **1238–1264 CE),** it represents the **culmination of Kalinga temple** **architecture**.
● The **Kanchi Kailasanathar temple** is the oldest structure in Kancheepuram dedicated to Lord Shiva. The Shiva lingam in this temple is 10 feet high. Kanchi Kailasanathar temple was **built in 7th century** **AD by a Pallava king Rajasimha** known as Narasimhavarman II. It is famous for its magnificent Vimana, the gopuram over the sanctum sanctorum. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following Jain texts:
I. - Sarvarthasiddhi
II. 	 Yogasastra
III. 	 Kalpa Sutra
IV.  	Acharanga Sutra
How many of the above were written in the Sanskrit language?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Jain literature** developed as a vast and rich tradition and is broadly divided into **Agam (canonical)** and **non-Agam (non-canonical)** works.
● **Sarvarthasiddhi** is the oldest commentary on the Tattvartha Sutra. It is written by Pujyapada in Sanskrit. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Yogasastra** is a treatise on the rules of conduct for laypeople and ascetics. It is written by Hemachandra in Sanskrit. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **Kalpa Sutra** is a **key Jain religious text**, traditionally attributed to **Bhadrabahu**. It contains the **biographies of Jain Tirthankaras**, including Mahavira, and lays down rules for monks and nuns. It is written in Ardhamagadhi Prakrit. **So, point III is not correct**.
● The **Acharanga Sutra** is the **foremost Ang Agam** and one of the oldest Jain canonical texts. It preserves the **direct teachings of Lord Mahavira**, especially on ascetic conduct, non-violence, and discipline. It is written in Ardhamagadhi Prakrit.  **So, point IV is not correct**. **So, only two of the above Jain texts are written in the Sanskrit language.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Jain Literature:**
● The Agams are regarded as the sacred teachings of **Lord Mahavira**, orally compiled by his disciples (Ganadharas) and later monks (Srut-kevalis) in **Ardha-Magadhi** **Prakrit**. They emphasise **non-violence, asceticism, vegetarianism, compassion,** **and reverence for life**.
● Over time, memorisation became difficult, leading to councils such as **Patliputra (4th** **century BC)** and **Valabhi (5th century AD)**, where many texts were standardised and eventually written down.
● While **Svetambara Jains** accept the surviving Agams as authentic, **Digambaras** believe the original Agams were lost and instead follow texts like the **Shatkhandagama** and **Kashayaprabhrta**, along with philosophical works arranged under **four Anuyogas** dealing with stories, conduct, cosmology, mathematics, and philosophy.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Carnatic music:
I. - Its origin can be traced to the Sama Veda.
II. 	 It is largely kriti-based, where compositions are set in a fixed raga and tala.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Carnatic music is an **indigenous classical music tradition of South India**, with greater emphasis on **vocal music and lyrical content (sahitya)**. It is largely **kriti-based**, where compositions are set in a **fixed raga and tala**, allowing structured yet limited improvisation. **So, statement II is correct**.
● The **origin of Carnatic music** can be traced back to the **Sama Veda**, which is regarded as the foundational source of Indian classical music. Vedic chanting gradually evolved from single-note recitation to structured scales of **seven swaras**, forming the basis of later classical traditions. **So, statement I is** **correct**.
● In Carnatic music, the term **‘Bani’** refers to a **particular style, school, or tradition of musical** **presentation**, shaped by lineage, pedagogy, and aesthetic approach. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Carnatic Music:**
● Unlike Hindustani music, Carnatic music has **less scope for free improvisation**, follows a **prescribed style**, and places **greater importance on tala (rhythm)**. It is organised around **72 Melakarta ragas**, from which numerous **janya ragas** are derived. The main accompanying instrument is the **mridangam**, while melodic instruments include **veena, violin, and mandolin**.
● In **Carnatic music**, **speed or tempo is referred to as ‘Kaala’**, and compositions are rendered in different kaalas such as **Prathama, Dviteeya, and Triteeya kaala**, reflecting increasing speed.
● Important elements include **Ragam** (melodic exposition without rhythm), **Tanam** (rhythmic melodic improvisation), and **Swara Kalpana** (improvised swara passages).
● Carnatic music was systematised by **Purandara Dasa**, known as the Pitamaha of Carnatic Music. Its golden age is associated with the **Trinity of Carnatic Music**— **Tyagaraja**, **Muthuswami Dikshitar**, and **Syama Sastri**, all born in **Thiruvarur**.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Ganeshwar
II. 	 Navdatoli
III. 	 Paiyampalli
IV. 	 Nevasa
V. 	 Jorwe
How many of the above are Chalcolithic sites in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Chalcolithic period** represents a **transitional phase** between the Neolithic and Bronze ages, marked by the use of **copper alongside stone tools.** In India, it broadly spans from about **2000 BC** **to 700 BC.**
● Chalcolithic cultures practiced **mixed economies** of agriculture, animal domestication, hunting, and fishing. They produced distinctive **painted pottery, lived in mud-brick houses**, and developed early metallurgy, especially **copper tools and ornaments.**
● **Major Chalcolithic sites** in India include Ahar, Balathal, Gilund, **Ganeshwar**, Inamgaon, Daimabad, **Nevasa**, **Jorwe**, **Navdatoli**, Eran, Kayatha, and Rangpur, associated with regional cultures such as **ahar–Banas, Malwa, Kayatha, and Jorwe. So, points I, II, IV and V are correct.**
● **Paiyampalli** village is located in the **Vellore** district of **Tamil Nadu**. It is famous for its excavation sites where the remains of the **Neolithic** and the **Megalithic** period were found. The excavations in Paiyampalli were carried out by the Archaeological Survey. The excavation was headed by **Shikaripura** **Ranganatha Rao** in 1964-65 and 1967-68. **So, point III is not correct.** **So, only four of the sites given above are predominantly Chalcolithic sites in India.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy

questions.questionText:
With reference to the Sangam economy, consider the following statements:
I. - There was a thriving industrial sector, with many crafts organised along hereditary lines.
II. 	 Irai and Tirai were important forms of revenue.
III. 	 Chattu refers to a caravan or a group of merchants travelling together.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Sangam Age (c. 300 BCE–300 CE)** in Tamilakam is known through **Sangam literature**, which provides rich details about s**ociety, polity, culture, and a diversified economy** based on agriculture, trade, crafts, and maritime contacts.
● While agriculture was the mainstay, the Sangam Age supported a **thriving industrial sector** where **crafts were often hereditary** and reached such a high level of expertise that their products were sought after in Rome and Southeast Asia. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ Sangam texts and archaeological evidence show **extensive overseas trade**, especially with the **Roman world.** Ports like **Kaveripattinam** handled exports of spices, pearls, textiles, and imports such as **Roman gold coins**, **wine**, and luxury goods.
● **Irai** referred to **land revenue or regular taxes c**ollected by rulers, while **Tirai** denoted tribute or **customs duties,** especially from trade and subordinate chiefs. These were **key sources of state** **income** in the Sangam economy. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Chattu** in the Sangam literature denotes a caravan or a group of merchants who travelled across regions with goods. They played an important role in **inland trade**, connecting rural producers with urban markets and ports. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Uraiyur, Kaveripattinam (Puhar), and Arikamedu** were major urban centres of **craft** **production and trade** during the Sangam age**.** **Arikamedu**, a premier Indo-Roman trading station near Pondicherry, specializes in **muslin**, **beads**, and **gems**. **Kaveripattinam** (Puhar), a bustling Chola port city with large-scale manufacturing, including **weaving** and **pottery**. **Uraiyur**, the capital of the early Cholas, is known as a major hub for **high-quality cotton** **textile** production.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Gupta period, consider the following statements:
I. - The practice of Sati gained wider social acceptance.
II. 	 The systematic practice of untouchability became a rigid social institution.
III. 	 The worship of Krishna as a major deity was started.
IV. 	 The influence of Buddhism declined in the Gangetic Valley.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Gupta period (4th–6th century CE)** is regarded as a classical age of Indian civilisation, marked by **Brahmanical revival**, **growth of Vaishnavism and Shaivism,** increased caste rigidity, and **gradual decline of Buddhism** in several regions, though not uniformly across India.
● **Evidence from inscriptions** and literary sources suggests that **Sati** began **gaining stronger social** **legitimacy** during the Gupta period. Though not universal, references indicate that **widow** **immolation** was increasingly glorified, especially among warrior and elite sections of society. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Groups like the Chandalas were considered impure as early as the 5th century BCE, so, untouchability did not begin during Gupta period but the **systematic practice and formal documentation** of untouchability (asprsya) became a rigid social institution during the **Gupta period. So, statement II** **is correct.**
● **Krishna worship** **began much earlier,** especially during the late Vedic and **post-Mauryan** period. The **Bhagavata** tradition and **Vasudeva-Krishna** tradition were already **prominent before the** **Guptas,** though Gupta rulers strongly patronised Vaishnavism and expanded Krishna devotion. **So,** **statement III is not correct.**
● During the Gupta period, **Buddhism declined in Northwestern India** due to reduced **patronage** and **competition** from Brahmanical traditions. However, it continued to remain **influential in the** **Gangetic Valley**, where major monasteries and **learning centres like Nalanda** flourished. **So,** **statement IV is not correct.** **So, only two of the statements given above are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Vijayanagara Empire & Bahmani Kingdom
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Dynasty/Kingdom Predecessor
l. Ikshvakus Satavahanas
II. - Satavahanas Mauryas
III. - Vijayanagara Hoysalas
IV. - Guptas Pushyabhuti
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Ikshvaku dynasty** emerged in the eastern Deccan (Andhra region) after the decline of the **Satavahanas** around the **3rd century CE.** They ruled from Vijayapuri (Nagarjunakonda) and patronised **Buddhism**, marking a regional political transition. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The **Satavahanas** rose to power in the Deccan after the fall of the **Mauryan** Empire. They asserted independence in the **post-Mauryan vacuum** and became the dominant power in peninsular India from the 1st century BCE to the 3rd century CE. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **Vijayanagara** Empire emerged in the 14th century CE following the decline of the **Hoysala** **kingdom** in Karnataka. Many Hoysala **administrative practices**, territories, and temple traditions were absorbed into the Vijayanagara state. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● The **Pushyabhuti dynasty (c. 500–647 CE),** also known as the **Vardhana** dynasty, succeeded the Gupta Empire in northern India, emerging as a major power in the **6th century after the decline of** **Gupta power.** They initially acted as feudatories to the Imperial Guptas and the Huns before establishing their independence in **Thanesar**. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs with reference to
medieval Southern India:
Term Description
I. - Kulakkil Administrative unit associated with irrigation tanks
II. - Anjuvannattar Maritime trading community along the West Coast
III. - Velaikkaran Personal bodyguards of the Emperor
IV. - Salabogam Land grants given to Brahmanas
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:**
● **Kulakkil** refers to **an administrative territorial unit found in the Chola Pandya regions,** often linked with irrigation structures such as tanks (eri). Along with units like nadu, kurram and valanadu, kulakkil functioned as a local division for revenue assessment and rural administration, reflecting the close relationship between agrarian organisation and water management in south Indian polity. **So, pair** **I is correctly matched**.
● **Anjuvannattar** were a powerful community of **maritime traders settled mainly in port towns** **along the western coast of South India**. They consisted of West Asians, Arabs, Jews, Christians and Muslims and played a key role in overseas trade. Over time, they merged with inland trading groups like the Manigramattar under large merchant guilds such as the Ainurruvar and Disai-ayirattu-ainurruvar,
which controlled long-distance maritime trade with Southeast Asia. **So, pair II is correctly matched**.
● **Velaikkaran** were the **elite personal bodyguards of the Chola emperors.** They formed a trusted military corps directly loyal to the king and were distinct from the regular army divisions. Their primary role was to protect the ruler and the royal household, symbolising the highly organised and hierarchical nature of the Chola military system. So, **pair III is correctly matched**.
● **Salabogam** refers to **land granted for the maintenance of schools**. The land grants given to Brahmans were called Brahmadeya. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched**. **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Iqta system:
I. - Iqtadars were originally military commanders
who were assigned the revenue of an area in
lieu of salary.
II. 	 Under Alauddin Khalji, Iqtadars were
permitted to appropriate surplus revenue for
their personal use.
III. 	 Firuz Shah Tughlaq made Iqtas hereditary.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The initial **Turkish conquests** in the early **13th century** displaced many local chiefs (whom the contemporary sources refer to as rai and rana).  In order to consolidate, the Turkish rulers made **revenue assignments (iqta),** in lieu of cash, to their **nobles (umara).**
● The **Iqta system** was a key administrative and revenue arrangement under the Delhi Sultanate. Revenue assignments (iqtas) were granted to nobles and military commanders known as **muqtis or iqtadars** in lieu of cash salaries. In return, they were required to maintain law and order in their assigned territories and supply troops to the Sultan. **So, statement I is correct**.
○ Over time, the system became central to governance and revenue collection. From the reign of Balban onwards, iqtadars were required to remit surplus revenue (fawazil) to the central treasury after meeting their sanctioned expenses, reflecting tighter central control.
● Under **Alauddin Khalji**, supervision was further strengthened. Detailed revenue assessments were introduced, audits were made stringent, transfers were frequent, and **surplus revenue was not** **allowed to be appropriated** for personal use. **So, statement II is not correct**.
○ Under **Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq (1320-25),** the **muqtis were allowed to keep l/10th to** **l/20th in excess of their sanctioned salaries.** The enhancements in the estimated revenue income by the central finance ministry were not to be more than 1/10 or 1/11th annually
● During the reign of **Firuz Shah Tughlaq**, central authority weakened and iqtas **gradually became** **hereditary**. This reduced the Sultan’s effective control over provincial administration. **So, statement** **III is correct**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Traveller Associated Ruler
I. - Al-Biruni Mahmud of Ghazni
II. - François Bernier Shah Jahan
III. - Niccolò de' Conti Deva Raya I
IV. - Abdur Razzaq Krishna Deva Raya
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● Accounts of foreign travellers provide valuable insights into India’s political, social, and cultural life in different historical periods. Their writings help historians reconstruct contemporary conditions, administrative systems, and economic activities.
● **Al-Biruni** (born 973 CE in Khwarizm) was taken to Ghazni after **Mahmud of Ghazni’s** conquest in 1017 CE. He learned Sanskrit and studied Indian society, religion, and science. His Kitab-ul-Hind provides a systematic account of Indian civilisation.  **So, pair I is correctly matched**.
● **François Bernier**, a French traveller, lived in India from 1656 to 1668 and served **Dara Shikoh**. He belonged to the Mughal period, not the early phase of European contacts. **So, pair II is not correctly** **matched**.
● **Nicolo de’ Conti**, an Italian traveller, visited Vijayanagara around 1420 during **Devaraya I’s** reign. His accounts describe its political and economic importance. **So, pair III is correctly matched**.
● **Abdur Razzaq**, a Persian ambassador, visited Vijayanagara during **Deva Raya II’s** reign and highlighted its prosperity, trade networks, and strong fortifications. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched**. **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - He was the founder of Pushtimarg.
II. 	 He emphasised devotion to child Krishna through Vatsalya Bhakti.
III. 	 He propounded the philosophy of Shuddhadvaita. Who among the following personalities is described above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **b**
**Explanation:**
● **Vallabhacharya**: He propounded the philosophy of **Suddhadvaita**, or pure non-dualism, through his commentaries on the Vedanta Sutra and the Bhagavad Gita. According to him, **Brahman is Sri Krishna**, who alone manifests as both the universe and individual souls. There is no real distinction between God, soul and matter; all are expressions of the same divine reality.
○ Liberation, therefore, does not lie in renunciation or knowledge alone but in **divine grace**. This path of devotion centred on Krishna came to be known as **Pushtimarga** (the path of grace). He emphasized unconditional love for Lord Krishna through **Vatsalya Bhakti (parental** **love/affection).**
● **Chaitanya:** Though a devotee of Krishna, he propounded Achintya Bheda Abheda (inconceivable oneness and difference) and is best known for Sankirtana (collective chanting), not for the doctrine of Shuddhadvaita.
● **Namdev:** A 14th-century saint of Maharashtra associated with the Varkari tradition, his hymns appear in the Guru Granth Sahib. However, he did not advocate Shuddhadvaita philosophy.
● **Ramanuja:** An 11th-century philosopher who propounded Vishishtadvaita (qualified non-dualism). While he provided a strong philosophical basis to Bhakti, his focus was on Vishnu/Narayana and a more institutionalised form of worship, rather than the “Path of Grace”. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Artist Contemporary
Ruler Work
I. - Vijnaneshvara Rajaraja
Narendra Mitakshara
II. - Nannayya Vikramaditya
VI
Andhra
Mahabharatam
III. - Allasani
Peddana Krishnadevaraya Manucharitramu
IV. - Somadeva Bhoja Kathasaritsagara In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **a**
**Explanation:**
● Indian literary and intellectual traditions were closely linked with royal patronage in the medieval period. Many scholars, poets, and jurists flourished under specific rulers, and their major works reflected the political and cultural environment of their times.
● **Vijnaneshvara** was associated with the court of **Vikramaditya VI (Western Chalukya ruler),** not Rajaraja Narendra. He authored the **Mitakshara**, a major commentary on Hindu law. **So, row I is not** **correctly matched**.
● **Nannayya** composed **Andhra Mahabharatam** under the patronage of **Rajaraja Narendra**, not Vikramaditya VI.  **Nannayya is revered as Adikavi,** or the first poet of Telugu. **So, row II is not** **correctly matched**.
○ Written around 1022 CE, the **Andhra Mahabharatam** represents the first major literary translation of the Sanskrit epic into Telugu and laid the foundation for classical Telugu poetry.
● **Allasani Peddana** flourished under **Krishnadevaraya** and authored **Manucharitramu**. He was one of the Ashtadiggajas of the Vijayanagara court. **So, row III is correctly matched**.
● **Somadeva** ( **11th-century Sanskrit poet**) was associated with the court of **King Ananta of** **Kashmir,** not Bhoja. He composed **Kathasaritsagara** based on the now-lost **Brhatkatha** of Gunadhya. **So, row IV is not correctly matched**. **So, in only one of the above rows is the given information correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to Thalassemia, consider the
following statements:
I. - It is an inherited blood disorder in which
the body is unable to produce sufficient
functional hemoglobin.
II. 	 If both parents are carriers of thalassemia,
there is a 100% chance that the child will
suffer from it.
III. 	 Its potential curative treatment includes bone
marrow/stem cell transplant.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Thalassemia** is a **hereditary hemoglobin disorder** caused by **genetic mutations** affecting **alpha** **or beta globin** chain production. Thalassemia **reduces the production of normal hemoglobin**,
which is essential for oxygen transport in red blood cells. This results in fewer healthy RBCs and **chronic** **anemia**, causing fatigue, breathlessness, and poor growth in severe cases. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Thalassemia is classified into **two main types**: **alpha and beta,** based on which **hemoglobin chain** is affected, further categorized by severity into **minor (trait), intermedia, and major.**
● If **both parents are carriers,** the probability follows **Mendelian inheritance: 25%** chance the child will have thalassemia major, **50% chance will be a carrier**, and **25% chance will be unaffected**. It is **not 100%. So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Blood transfusion** is a **major treatment**, **not a cure.** The potential curative option is **bone** **marrow/stem cell transplant**, though it is risky and requires compatible donors. **New gene therapy** **approaches** are also being explored. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Scientists recently tested a gene-editing method called adenine base editing to fix two severe mutations that cause β-thalassemia, a genetic blood disease.

questions.subject: Economy

questions.questionText:
Project Stargate is a joint venture led by which of
the following organisations?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Project Stargate** is a large-scale AI infrastructure initiative aimed at developing advanced artificial intelligence systems in the United States with a total investment of up to $500 billion over several years. It is a co-led joint venture with SoftBank as chairman and OpenAI as the operational lead.
● The project involves constructing massive AI data centers and campuses across the United States to strengthen AI research, innovation, and national technological leadership.
● It aims to create a substantial number of jobs and bolster U.S. competitiveness in AI, especially in comparison with global rivals. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance: Project Stargate** was announced on January 21, 2025, by U.S. President at the White House, alongside industry leaders from OpenAI, SoftBank, and Oracle.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to phosphine (PH₃), consider the
following statements:
I. - It is an extremely toxic and flammable gas
used mainly as a fumigant for stored grains.
II. 	 Its detection in planetary atmospheres is
considered a potential biosignature.
III. 	 It is commonly used as a refrigerant due to its
low solubility in water.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Phosphine** is a **colourless, flammable, and extremely toxic gas** with a characteristic **garlic- or** **fish-like odour**.
○ It is widely used as a **fumigant to control insect pests** in stored grains and other food commodities. **So, statement I is correct.**
● It is chemically known as **hydrogen phosphide** and is structurally similar to **ammonia (NH**₃**)**, though it is much less soluble in water.
● Phosphine is produced by the **reaction of water with calcium phosphide** or by treating white phosphorus with hot water or a strong base.
● The presence of phosphine in planetary atmospheres (such as Venus) has been discussed as a **possible** **biosignature**, since on Earth it is mainly associated with biological or anthropogenic activity. So, **statement II is correct.**
● Phosphine is **extremely toxic** and flammable; it is **not used as a refrigerant. So, statement III is** **not correct.**
● It acts as a **metabolic poison**, disrupting aerobic respiration and causing oxidative stress. There is **no** **effective antidote**, and poisoning can lead to severe organ damage or death.. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the toxicology report by the Indian Veterinary Research Institute has found phosphines in the animals' rumen and intestines.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to Nipah Virus (NiV) infection, consider the following statements:
I. - It is a zoonotic disease.
II. 	 Its symptoms include acute respiratory illness and encephalitis.
III. 	 There are currently no approved drugs or vaccines for this infection.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Nipah virus (NiV)** is a highly fatal **zoonotic** infection caused by a **Henipavirus**. It spreads from **animals to human**s and also through **human-to-human** transmission. It can cause respiratory illness and encephalitis with **high mortality.**
● Nipah virus is a **zoonotic** disease, meaning it is transmitted from **animals to humans.** It is caused by a **Henipavirus**. **Fruit bats (Pteropus species)** are the natural reservoir. **Pigs** and other animals may act as **intermediate** hosts during outbreaks. **So, statement I is correct.**
● It is **not transmitted only through direct** animal contact. Humans can also get infected by consuming **contaminated food** such as **raw date palm sap** or fruits contaminated by **bat saliva**, **urine**, or **droppings**.
● It can cause **fever, cough, breathing difficulty**, and **acute respiratory distress**. In severe cases, it affects the **nervous** system, leading to **encephalitis**, causing confusion, **seizures, coma**, and high fatality rates.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● There are currently **no approved vaccines or specific antiviral drugs** for Nipah virus infection. Treatment mainly involves supportive care such as **oxygen support,** intensive monitoring, and management of **neurological complications**. WHO lists Nipah as a priority disease. **So, statement** **III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Two suspected Nipah virus infections among healthcare workers in West Bengal have triggered an urgent state and central public health response.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText: With regard to ricin, a potent toxin recently seen in the news, which one of the following statements is **not** correct?

options.a: It is a by-product of the industrial manufacturing of castor oil.
options.b: It functions at the cellular level by inhibiting protein synthesis through the inactivation of ribosomes.
options.c: There is no globally approved vaccine or specific medical antidote for it.
options.d: It is listed under the Biological Weapons Convention but is excluded from the Chemical Weapons Convention.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ricin** is a **highly potent protein toxin** obtained from the seeds of the **castor bean plant (****Ricinus** **communis****)**.
● It is released mainly as a **by-product (waste mash/chaff)** during the industrial extraction of **castor** **oil**.
● Ricin is classified as a **Type II ribosome-inactivating protein (RIP)** and exerts toxicity by **blocking** **protein synthesis**, leading to cell death.
● The toxin can enter the body through **ingestion, inhalation, or injection**, with extremely small doses capable of being lethal.
● **No globally approved vaccine or specific antidote** for ricin poisoning exists; treatment is largely **supportive and symptomatic**.
● It is a controlled chemical under **Schedule 1A of the Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC)**, and also included under the **Biological Weapons Convention (BWC).** **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Gujarat Anti-Terrorist Squad (ATS) has investigated an alleged ricin chemical poison terror plot.
